questions.question: A media platform uses generative models to produce large volumes of content recommendations and short summaries. Manual review for fairness and stereotyping has become impractical, but the trust and safety team still needs a consistent evaluation approach that identifies biased or unfair patterns across demographic groups with periodic human spot checks. As the responsible AI specialist, how would you design an automated judging system that evaluates fairness, reduces judge model bias, and remains aligned with human reviewers?

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to block outputs that contain demographic references or potentially sensitive phrases. Enable CloudWatch alarms so reviewers can examine filtered responses and treat filter triggers as evidence of fairness compliance
choices.explanation: Guardrails help filter unsafe content but do not generate fairness scores or identify demographic bias in generated text. Treating guardrail triggers as fairness evidence overlooks subtle stereotyping patterns that require structured evaluation through judge models.

choices.text: Design an LLM as a judge workflow by defining structured fairness rubrics and evaluating each generated output through Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flows that orchestrate fairness scoring. Use ensemble judging across multiple Bedrock models and regularly calibrate judge scores against a human labeled dataset to reduce bias and maintain reviewer alignment
choices.explanation: Design an LLM as a judge workflow by defining structured fairness rubrics and evaluating each generated output through Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flows that orchestrate fairness scoring. Use ensemble judging across multiple Bedrock models and regularly calibrate judge scores against a human labeled dataset to reduce bias and maintain reviewer alignment A structured fairness rubric evaluated through Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flows provides a consistent and repeatable method for fairness scoring across diverse outputs. Using multiple Bedrock models as an ensemble strengthens the integrity of the evaluation by reducing single model bias and producing more stable judgments. Human calibration is required to maintain alignment with real reviewer expectations as model behavior evolves. Comparing automated judge outputs to human labeled samples ensures the fairness evaluation pipeline remains reliable and effective at identifying subtle stereotyping patterns. Here is a deep-dive on using LLM-as-a-judge for Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/llm-as-a-judge-on-amazon-bedrock-model-evaluation/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/llm-as-a-judge-on-amazon-bedrock-model-evaluation/

choices.text: Create an LLM as a judge workflow by defining fairness scoring rubrics and evaluating each output through Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flows that run a single high performing foundation model. Periodically compare judge results with historical fairness thresholds and adjust scoring prompts instead of performing calibration with human labeled data
choices.explanation: Adjusting scoring prompts based on historical thresholds cannot replace human calibration, which is required to validate fairness judgments against real reviewer standards. Running only a single model inside Prompt Flows also increases exposure to judge model bias because ensemble diversity is not used.

choices.text: Implement a fairness judging pipeline by defining scoring prompts and routing generated outputs through a single model in Amazon Bedrock Prompt Flows. Leverage CloudWatch metrics to validate judge consistency over time instead of performing calibration with human labeled samples
choices.explanation: Routing outputs through a single Bedrock model and relying on CloudWatch metrics does not provide sufficient protection against systematic bias. Lack of human calibration prevents verification that automated judgments remain aligned with fairness expectations.

questions.question: A global retailer wants to build a GenAI customer support assistant that responds to users in real time through a web and mobile app. The assistant must answer natural language questions, call internal APIs to fetch order status or refund eligibility, and sometimes trigger workflows to update CRM records. The engineering team wants a managed orchestration approach that can maintain conversation context and return responses within a few seconds. Which orchestration approach should the solutions architect recommend to coordinate the GenAI model, tools, and downstream actions for this real time assistant?

choices.text: Orchestrate each user request using a Step Functions Express workflow that is triggered by API Gateway. Load conversation history in the workflow and call Bedrock as well as internal APIs through Lambda. Return the response after the state machine finishes
choices.explanation: This option uses AWS Step Functions Express Workflows as the primary orchestrator for each conversational request. Express workflows support high throughput and relatively low latency, and they integrate well with Lambda, Bedrock, and other AWS services. However, Step Functions focuses on workflow state transitions rather than fine grained conversational tool use and agentic reasoning. The architect would need to manually implement conversation state handling, tool selection, and multi step reasoning logic inside the state machine and Lambda tasks, which adds complexity for an interactive assistant that must respond in a few seconds. In addition, Step Functions introduces extra overhead compared to calling an agent directly, which works against the strict latency requirements for real time chat.

choices.text: Provision an agent using Amazon Bedrock Agents and define action groups that call Lambda functions wrapping internal order and CRM APIs. Expose the agent through API Gateway so the assistant can maintain conversation context, and respond in real time
choices.explanation: Provision an agent using Amazon Bedrock Agents and define action groups that call Lambda functions wrapping internal order and CRM APIs. Expose the agent through API Gateway so the assistant can maintain conversation context, and respond in real time This option uses Amazon Bedrock Agents as the managed orchestration layer that coordinates the foundation model, tool calls, and conversation context. Agents in Bedrock can maintain multi turn conversations, automatically decide when to call tools, and orchestrate interactions with downstream systems through action groups. You define action groups that map to Lambda functions or HTTP endpoints which wrap internal order status APIs, refund logic, or CRM workflows. The agent uses these action groups to autonomously call tools when user queries require actions, then combines tool responses with model reasoning to produce a final natural language answer. Exposing the agent through Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda gives web and mobile clients a low latency, stateless interface while keeping orchestration inside Bedrock Agents. API Gateway can front a REST or WebSocket API for interactive applications, while the agent core runtime remains serverless and managed. This pattern matches AWS guidance for using Bedrock to build agents that integrate with tools and business systems, and it aligns with the need for near real time responses for conversational assistants. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/agents.html

choices.text: Send each chat message into EventBridge and start a Step Functions Standard workflow that fans out to Lambda tasks calling Bedrock and internal APIs. Clients poll another API to retrieve the final aggregated response after the workflow completes
choices.explanation: This option uses Amazon EventBridge and Step Functions Standard Workflows with a polling pattern, which fits asynchronous event processing much better than low latency conversational flows. Standard workflows introduce higher startup latency and are optimized for long running or durable processes instead of sub second or few second interactive responses. Persisting intermediate data in S3 and asking clients to poll for results increases end to end latency and complicates the client experience, which conflicts directly with the requirement for real time responses in a chat interface. The event driven pattern and batch style orchestration do not match the primary skill area of designing low latency GenAI workflows for interactive assistants.

choices.text: Call the Bedrock model directly from a Lambda function and manually embed order and CRM data into prompts. Use a Lambda function to store and reload conversation history from DynamoDB and construct all logic without any agent or tool orchestration
choices.explanation: This option builds a custom orchestration layer entirely inside a single Lambda function and treats Amazon Bedrock as a basic text generation API. While this approach can work for very simple use cases, it ignores the managed tool calling and agent features that Bedrock provides. The developer must write custom code to parse user intent, decide which internal APIs to call, manage prompt construction, and stitch together model responses with tool outputs. This manual orchestration increases complexity and reduces flexibility compared to using Bedrock Agents, which are designed to integrate tools and handle multi turn interactions with less custom glue logic.

questions.question: A GenAI documentation assistant is used by hundreds of internal teams, and cost reports show wide variance in token usage across different workloads. Finance requires accurate chargeback visibility across teams, projects, and environments, and leadership wants strict token budgets for experimental workloads while still permitting higher limits for mission critical applications. As the lead GenAI engineer, how would you design a token estimation and tracking mechanism that enforces per tenant token budgets while allowing prioritized workloads to exceed preset thresholds when necessary?

choices.text: Implement a token accounting layer that records estimated token usage for each request in Amazon DynamoDB and forwards periodic summaries to Amazon CloudWatch Logs for reporting. Use AWS Lambda to soft enforce token budgets by allowing all requests to proceed while tagging overage attempts for later review
choices.explanation: Recording estimated token usage without enforcing real time limits does not satisfy the need for strict per tenant budgets or controlled exceptions for critical workloads. Soft enforcement allows excessive token consumption to continue unchecked, which prevents predictable spending and undermines chargeback accuracy across environments. Relying on retrospective reviews does not provide the operational guardrails needed for large multi team GenAI systems.

choices.text: Create a token metering layer that logs input and output tokens for every request through Amazon API Gateway and stores these metrics in Amazon CloudWatch and Amazon DynamoDB for per tenant reporting. Use AWS Lambda to enforce token budgets by evaluating usage before calling Amazon Bedrock and allow elevated limits for critical workloads through configurable policy rules
choices.explanation: Create a token metering layer that logs input and output tokens for every request through Amazon API Gateway and stores these metrics in Amazon CloudWatch and Amazon DynamoDB for per tenant reporting. Use AWS Lambda to enforce token budgets by evaluating usage before calling Amazon Bedrock and allow elevated limits for critical workloads through configurable policy rules A structured token metering layer provides the visibility and control required for accurate chargebacks and per tenant governance. Logging input and output tokens at the API boundary ensures consistent measurement across all workloads, and storing metrics in DynamoDB and CloudWatch enables granular dashboards for teams, projects, and environments. Budget enforcement logic at the gateway or Lambda layer ensures that experimental workloads remain constrained while mission critical workloads are governed through approved exceptions. Configurable policies allow specific tenants or workloads to exceed standard limits in a controlled manner without compromising financial management or operational consistency. This design ensures predictable spending, supports fair allocation of GenAI costs, and provides the flexibility leaders need for high value workloads.

choices.text: Aggregate cost data from the AWS Billing and Cost Management console and rely on periodic cost allocation tags to approximate per tenant token consumption. Configure Amazon EventBridge rules to notify teams when overall monthly spending trends exceed predefined thresholds
choices.explanation: Billing and cost allocation tags do not provide token level granularity and cannot accurately measure consumption patterns at the team or project level. This approach lacks real time enforcement capabilities and cannot support fine grained token budgets or differentiated limits for critical workloads.

choices.text: Build a centralized usage tracker that records token estimates in DynamoDB but allow teams to bypass budget checks by passing a priority flag in their API request. Use a simple Lambda based interceptor to send warnings when teams exceed token budgets instead of enforcing hard limits
choices.explanation: Allowing teams to bypass budget enforcement with a priority flag weakens governance and leads to unbounded token consumption. Sending warnings without applying hard limits provides no real control, and the system becomes unable to enforce chargeback accountability across teams and environments.

questions.question: An educational company has a multi-step GenAI pipeline that generates quiz questions from course materials, validates them, and publishes them to a learning platform. The pipeline sometimes fails due to malformed source content, model timeouts, or quota issues. The company wants end to end visibility into where failures occur, automatic retries with backoff for transient errors, routing of permanently failed items to a dead-letter queue, and centralized logs and metrics for troubleshooting. How would you orchestrate the GenAI workflow to provide resilience, observability, and targeted error handling across all steps?

choices.text: Create an Amazon SQS queue that feeds a fleet of EC2 worker instances running a custom GenAI processing script. Push application logs to CloudWatch Logs for troubleshooting
choices.explanation: Using SQS with EC2 workers can process tasks reliably, but it forces the team to build all error handling, retries, and monitoring logic manually inside scripts. If a worker crashes or fails during a specific GenAI step, the system has no native view of which stage caused the error or how many items failed in each stage. This approach increases operational burden and does not offer the structured state tracking, per step retries, or visual debugging that a managed orchestrator provides.

choices.text: Implement an AWS Step Functions Standard workflow that wraps each GenAI step in states with Retry for transient errors and Catch that routes unrecoverable failures to an SQS dead letter queue. Enable CloudWatch Logs and Metrics for the state machine and integrate AWS X Ray tracing on Lambda tasks and model calls to gain full visibility into latency and failure patterns
choices.explanation: Implement an AWS Step Functions Standard workflow that wraps each GenAI step in states with Retry for transient errors and Catch that routes unrecoverable failures to an SQS dead letter queue. Enable CloudWatch Logs and Metrics for the state machine and integrate AWS X Ray tracing on Lambda tasks and model calls to gain full visibility into latency and failure patterns Use AWS Step Functions Standard workflows as the backbone for resilient, observable GenAI orchestration. Step Functions provides native constructs for Retry with exponential backoff and Catch handlers, which makes it ideal for modeling transient issues such as model timeouts or throttling separately from permanent failures like malformed source content. Each state in the workflow can wrap a specific stage of the pipeline, for example question generation, validation, and publishing, and can define tailored retry policies on particular error codes from Lambda, Bedrock, or other integrated services. Non recoverable errors can flow into a dedicated Amazon SQS dead letter queue, where failed items are stored with contextual metadata for later inspection or reprocessing. On the observability side, enable CloudWatch Logs and Metrics for the state machine and configure built in Step Functions metrics such as executions started, succeeded, failed, throttled, and timed out, then add alarms for anomalous error rates. For deeper tracing across Lambda functions and downstream calls, activate AWS X Ray so that each execution can be followed end to end, including GenAI model calls, validation logic, and publishing steps. This design yields a clear, graphical view of where failures occur, enforces structured error handling across all steps, and uses SQS DLQs to isolate permanently failed items without impacting the rest of the pipeline.

choices.text: Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger a series of independent Lambda functions chained by events. Configure each Lambda function to write status messages into an S3 bucket. Leverage ad hoc log searches in CloudWatch Logs and manual inspection of the S3 status files to identify and retry failed items
choices.explanation: Chaining events through EventBridge and multiple Lambda functions creates a loosely connected workflow that lacks centralized control. Each function runs independently, so errors and status information end up scattered across logs and S3 files, making the pipeline difficult to trace or debug. Without unified workflow visibility, targeted retries, or built in error handling rules, the team must perform manual investigation and custom coding to manage failures.

choices.text: Place all GenAI logic into a single Lambda function that processes each item end to end. Leverage Lambda automatic retries and a dead letter queue for failed invocations. Configure basic CloudWatch alarms on function errors for monitoring and observability
choices.explanation: Putting the entire GenAI pipeline inside one Lambda function makes the architecture simple, but it hides all internal steps and makes it hard to see where failures occur. Lambda’s built in retry and DLQ features only operate at the function level, so the team cannot distinguish whether generation, validation, or publishing failed. This approach provides minimal observability and no structured way to apply different retry rules to different failure types, which limits control and troubleshooting.

questions.question: A marketing content assistant recently adopted a new “tone and brand” prompt template that was manually tuned in a staging environment. After rollout, some product lines show improved copy while others regress, and the team has no structured way to compare the new template against the previous one across a broad test set. How should you introduce a prompt testing framework and evaluation workflow to systematically compare prompt versions and prevent quality regressions?

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock to execute both prompt versions on a small set of sample inputs and manually compare the outputs for tone and brand alignment. Save the sample outputs to Amazon S3 and perform occasional reviews when product teams raise concerns
choices.explanation: Testing only on a small input subset and relying on manual review misses regressions that appear only on broader product lines or edge-case phrasing. Without automated evaluation or consistent scoring, this approach cannot ensure stable quality across large and diverse catalogs.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock model evaluation jobs to run both the legacy and updated prompt templates across a comprehensive validation dataset and capture structured quality metrics. Store prompt versions and evaluation outputs in Amazon DynamoDB so that you can automate periodic comparisons and detect regressions consistently
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock model evaluation jobs to run both the legacy and updated prompt templates across a comprehensive validation dataset and capture structured quality metrics. Store prompt versions and evaluation outputs in Amazon DynamoDB so that you can automate periodic comparisons and detect regressions consistently Running structured evaluation jobs using Amazon Bedrock allows you to test both prompt versions on the same dataset using consistent, repeatable conditions. This produces measurable metrics such as clarity, tone, consistency, and adherence to brand guidelines, allowing you to clearly identify regressions or improvements. Storing both prompt versions and evaluation results in DynamoDB gives you a durable and queryable history of performance across model upgrades, dataset changes, or prompt refinements. This combination provides a scalable prompt-testing pipeline that supports continuous quality assurance. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/evaluation.html

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock model evaluation jobs to test only the updated prompt template against a curated dataset while retaining the previous template as a baseline stored in DynamoDB. Review the evaluation outputs periodically and adjust the new template based on high level scoring trends rather than running side by side comparisons
choices.explanation: Evaluating only the new template and not performing parallel tests against the previous version prevents true regression detection, because you cannot measure relative performance. High level scoring metrics without paired comparisons miss subtle degradations in tone, coverage, or brand alignment that only appear when the two versions are evaluated under identical conditions.

choices.text: Adopt the new prompt template only for recently introduced products and phase it into older product lines over time. Use S3 object versioning to track prompt templates and assume the new configuration maintains quality across product segments without structured testing
choices.explanation: Incrementally applying new prompts without systematic evaluation risks inconsistent tone across product lines. S3 versioning provides historical tracking but does not evaluate or validate output quality.

questions.question: A legal tech startup uses an FM on Amazon Bedrock to extract risk tagged summaries from long contract documents. They are preparing a new version of their Bedrock prompt template to improve clarity but are concerned that the update may miss certain sensitive clauses that the previous version consistently detected. The team wants a repeatable regression testing framework where both the old and new prompt templates run against a large set of historical contract documents, produce structured outputs, and generate comparison metrics that highlight improvements or regressions before a prompt version is promoted. As the GenAI lead, how should you design this regression testing workflow using AWS services?

choices.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to evaluate the semantic similarity between old and new prompt outputs and store the similarity scores in S3. Run the regression workflow through Lambda without orchestrating multi step comparisons
choices.explanation: Amazon Comprehend cannot replace structured regression logic because semantic similarity scoring alone does not verify whether risk clauses are missing or misclassified. Running everything through Lambda without orchestration limits the ability to perform controlled multi stage testing across a large corpus.

choices.text: Use Step Functions to coordinate a pipeline that runs both prompt versions on the contract corpus and store all outputs in DynamoDB for comparison. Use a Lambda function to trigger new prompt version updates automatically whenever regression metrics indicate improvements
choices.explanation: This option is incorrect because DynamoDB is not designed to store large contract summaries or structured comparison datasets at scale. Automatically updating prompt versions based on regression metrics removes the required manual approval and governance steps that are critical for legal and compliance driven workloads.

choices.text: Use Step Functions to orchestrate a test workflow that runs both prompt versions on the same contract corpus, store all outputs in S3, and use a Lambda function to compare results and generate regression metrics. Publish the comparison data to CloudWatch Metrics to track improvements or degradations over time
choices.explanation: Use Step Functions to orchestrate a test workflow that runs both prompt versions on the same contract corpus, store all outputs in S3, and use a Lambda function to compare results and generate regression metrics. Publish the comparison data to CloudWatch Metrics to track improvements or degradations over time This approach uses Step Functions to coordinate a controlled regression test workflow where both old and new prompts run on the same dataset, ensuring consistent and repeatable evaluation. Storing the outputs in S3 and comparing them with a Lambda function allows the team to compute structured regression metrics that quantify differences across clauses and summaries. Publishing the metrics to CloudWatch allows the team to monitor trend changes in prompt quality and identify regressions before releasing new versions. This design aligns with best practices for automated prompt evaluation and supports scalable and maintainable regression testing workflows.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to automatically run both prompt versions on the entire corpus and update template versions based on output comparisons. Store the regression test results in DynamoDB and visualize them directly through Guardrails analytics
choices.explanation: Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management does not run regression testing automatically and cannot evaluate template performance without external workflows. Guardrails analytics cannot visualize prompt regression metrics, and prompt updates must follow approvals rather than automatic version promotion.

questions.question: A large ecommerce business processes thousands of refund requests every day, each involving different rules, exceptions, and special conditions. Refund decisions often require evaluating complex policy criteria, checking the latest order and delivery information, and determining whether an issue should be resolved automatically or escalated to a specialist. The customer support team wants to automate this reasoning process using an intelligent system that can think through the policy logic step by step, call internal order systems when needed, and repeat this cycle until it reaches a clear decision. Which approach should you implement to enable structured reasoning and action execution in an iterative loop until a final refund decision is reached?

choices.text: Design an AWS Step Functions workflow that alternates between a reasoning state powered by a foundation model and an action state that invokes downstream APIs, implementing the ReAct pattern. Configure the workflow to repeat the reasoning and action cycle until the model reaches a final approval or escalation decision
choices.explanation: Design an AWS Step Functions workflow that alternates between a reasoning state powered by a foundation model and an action state that invokes downstream APIs, implementing the ReAct pattern. Configure the workflow to repeat the reasoning and action cycle until the model reaches a final approval or escalation decision This approach correctly implements the ReAct pattern by separating reasoning from action execution and orchestrating them as alternating states in an AWS Step Functions workflow. The foundation model performs structured reasoning in one state, then the workflow transitions to an action state that calls internal systems such as order services or refund validation APIs. This clear separation allows the model to reconsider its reasoning after each action and refine the workflow toward an accurate decision. By repeating the reasoning action loop until the model determines that it has all the information required to approve a refund or route it for escalation, the workflow supports iterative refinement. This design ensures predictable execution, controlled decision making, and high quality outcomes for complex refund evaluations. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/database/key-components-of-a-data-driven-agentic-ai-application/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/database/key-components-of-a-data-driven-agentic-ai-application/

choices.text: Use AWS Glue workflows to coordinate reasoning steps and API calls and store intermediate reasoning outputs in S3. Configure Glue triggers to execute each stage of the refund evaluation process sequentially
choices.explanation: AWS Glue workflows are designed for data processing pipelines and are not suitable for orchestrating iterative reasoning loops or coordinating model driven decisions.

choices.text: Use a single Amazon Bedrock Agent with multiple action groups that handle both reasoning and API calls within one step. Configure the agent to follow policy prompts and rely on its internal reasoning to reach a final decision without an orchestrated reasoning action loop
choices.explanation: Relying on a single Bedrock Agent to handle both reasoning and actions within a single step does not meet the requirement of an iterative structure. Without explicit state separation or repeated reasoning action cycles, the agent cannot refine its decision based on newly retrieved API results.

choices.text: Design a Step Functions workflow that uses a single foundation model state to perform both reasoning and API execution in one step. Configure the workflow to run only once per request without iterative reasoning or repeated API calls
choices.explanation: Combining reasoning and action in a single state does not meet the requirement of an iterative loop. Without repeated cycles of reasoning and tool execution, the workflow cannot refine decisions or request additional information when needed.

questions.question: An e commerce company uses a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock to generate product recommendations and is evaluating a new candidate model that might improve conversion rates. The company wants to run an online A/B test that sends a small percentage of traffic to the candidate model, tracks detailed metrics for each model, and optionally combines outputs with an ensemble strategy before responding to the user. How should you design the A/B testing and ensemble routing pattern for these foundation models?

choices.text: Use Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda to randomly route about 5 to 10 percent of requests to the candidate Bedrock model while sending the rest to the existing model, driven by a traffic split configuration stored in AWS AppConfig. Tag each request with the selected model identifier and record conversion, latency, and quality metrics per model in Amazon CloudWatch and Amazon DynamoDB, optionally running ensemble logic in the Lambda function that can be enabled or disabled without redeploying
choices.explanation: Use Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda to randomly route about 5 to 10 percent of requests to the candidate Bedrock model while sending the rest to the existing model, driven by a traffic split configuration stored in AWS AppConfig. Tag each request with the selected model identifier and record conversion, latency, and quality metrics per model in Amazon CloudWatch and Amazon DynamoDB, optionally running ensemble logic in the Lambda function that can be enabled or disabled without redeploying This solution uses Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda at the API layer to implement a controlled random traffic split, for example 5 to 10 percent of requests to the candidate Bedrock model and the rest to the current model. Storing the traffic split configuration in AWS AppConfig allows you to adjust experiment percentages without code changes, and tagging each request with the chosen model identifier makes it possible to attribute conversion and latency metrics accurately per model. Recording metrics in CloudWatch and structured data in DynamoDB gives you both time series views and detailed per request or per user views for robust analysis. Implementing ensemble logic inside Lambda lets you combine model outputs when needed, for example by weighted ranking or tie breaking, and turn this behavior on or off via configuration so you can run pure A/B tests or hybrid modes. This design meets all requirements: controlled online experimentation, clear attribution, safe rollout and rollback, and the ability to promote the winning model with minimal disruption.

choices.text: Use Amazon Route 53 weighted routing to send 10 percent of traffic to a separate API endpoint that fronts the candidate Bedrock model and 90 percent to the existing endpoint, without any additional routing logic in the application. Rely on CloudWatch aggregated metrics at the endpoint level and manually compare overall performance to decide when to switch all traffic to the candidate model
choices.explanation: This option uses Route 53 weighted routing, which is appropriate for splitting traffic between independent stacks, but it only operates at the DNS or endpoint level and does not provide per request model attribution within the application. Because both endpoints front recommendation services, CloudWatch metrics will be aggregated at the service level unless you add extra tagging, and manually comparing overall endpoint performance is coarse grained and error prone for A/B testing. This design also makes it harder to implement ensemble logic, since routing decisions and experiment configuration live outside the application layer where model responses are combined.

choices.text: Update the recommendation service to route all online traffic to the candidate Bedrock model during the test period while periodically invoking the original model in an offline batch job for comparison. Store experiment results in Amazon S3 and use scheduled AWS Glue jobs to compute conversion statistics before deciding whether to roll back or keep the candidate model
choices.explanation: This option sends all live traffic to the candidate model, which turns the evaluation into a full production cutover rather than a controlled A/B test, and relies only on offline batch comparisons to decide whether to roll back. This approach does not meet the requirement to send only a small percentage of traffic to the candidate model and also introduces higher risk, because any regression in the candidate model affects every user until you manually react. While S3 and Glue are appropriate for offline analytics, they do not provide the fine grained online routing, per request attribution, and low risk incremental rollout that the question is asking for.

choices.text: Use Amazon API Gateway with a Lambda authorizer that reads a feature flag from AWS AppConfig and calls both Bedrock models for every request, then returns the candidate model output to the client while storing the primary model output only for offline analysis. Aggregate all performance metrics into a single CloudWatch metric namespace without including the model identifier to simplify reporting
choices.explanation: This option calls both models for every request and always returns the candidate model’s output, which is not a true A/B test and doubles your cost and latency. Aggregating all performance data into a single CloudWatch metric namespace without a model identifier mixes metrics from both models, which prevents you from computing accurate per model conversion rates or diagnosing issues specific to one model.

questions.question: A large enterprise has centralized all foundation model inference inside a dedicated AI platform account that runs Amazon Bedrock models behind a controlled access layer. Individual business units maintain their own Amazon OpenSearch Serverless vector collections and Aurora PostgreSQL clusters with the pgvector extension in separate AWS accounts. A new initiative requires the Bedrock based assistant in the AI platform account to retrieve context directly from these remote vector indexes without duplicating embeddings or moving the underlying data into the AI platform. The enterprise must preserve strict cross account security boundaries, minimize operational overhead, and still support efficient vector similarity search for retrieval augmented generation. What is the most effective way to design this cross account retrieval architecture?

choices.text: Export all embeddings and source metadata from each business unit into Amazon S3 in the AI platform account and rebuild new OpenSearch Serverless vector collections centrally. Then schedule periodic Lambda sync jobs to refresh the centralized collections as new documents arrive
choices.explanation: Rebuilding vector indexes in the AI platform account duplicates large datasets and increases storage and ingestion costs. This design also introduces synchronization delays and violates best practices for minimizing data movement across accounts.

choices.text: Build a single Amazon API Gateway endpoint in the AI platform account that proxies requests to all business unit indexes across accounts. Then require each FM application to pass an index identifier so the gateway can forward the query to the correct backend
choices.explanation: Using a central API Gateway proxy introduces a single point of routing, weakens fine grained access control, and forces request level filtering to determine the correct index. This breaks isolation guarantees across business units and increases operational overhead.

choices.text: Expose the business unit vector indexes through cross account IAM permissions supported by OpenSearch Serverless or Aurora pgvector and connect the AI platform account using AWS PrivateLink for secure, private retrieval. Then allow the Bedrock based assistant to issue vector similarity queries on demand while the data remains in the owner accounts
choices.explanation: Expose the business unit vector indexes through cross account IAM permissions supported by OpenSearch Serverless or Aurora pgvector and connect the AI platform account using AWS PrivateLink for secure, private retrieval. Then allow the Bedrock based assistant to issue vector similarity queries on demand while the data remains in the owner accounts This approach uses AWS PrivateLink and cross account IAM permissions to let the AI platform query remote vector indexes without duplicating data or violating account level separation. OpenSearch Serverless supports cross account access using the assume role option, and Aurora pgvector supports cross account retrieval through secure VPC connectivity and IAM authentication when combined with controlled API exposure. This design aligns with the requirements for secure multi account retrieval, optimal vector search performance, and minimal data movement. It preserves data ownership within each business unit while enabling efficient vector similarity search for retrieval augmented generation in the Bedrock environment.

choices.text: Ingest all business unit documents into a consolidated Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base inside the AI platform and let Bedrock generate new embeddings for all content. Then remove the need for cross account connectivity entirely by relying only on the consolidated Knowledge Base for retrieval
choices.explanation: Reingesting all business unit documents into a consolidated Bedrock Knowledge Base forces a full re-embedding of content, increases duplication, and breaks the design principle of keeping vector stores close to their owners. It removes autonomy from the business units and complicates governance for large multi account environments.

questions.question: An HR SaaS provider is developing multiple GenAI capabilities on Amazon Bedrock, including job description generation, interview question drafting, and performance review summarization. Product teams have begun creating and modifying system prompts independently, resulting in inconsistent tone, duplicated effort, and uncontrolled changes moving into production. Leadership wants to establish a centralized prompt catalog with parameterized templates, role based approval workflows, and full auditability of prompt changes across teams. As a GenAI developer, how should you implement prompt management and governance so that teams can safely reuse and evolve prompts?

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to store prompt templates and allow teams to update versions automatically when changes are detected. Use CloudWatch to record which prompt versions are used in production
choices.explanation: Although this option uses Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management, it is incorrect because prompt updates cannot be applied automatically based on change detection. Prompt Management requires controlled approvals and version promotion, which automatic update workflows would violate.

choices.text: Store all prompt templates in S3 and use AWS IAM permissions to control which teams can modify them. Enable CloudTrail to track file access and rely on Lambda functions to validate template structure
choices.explanation: S3 and IAM can control access, but they cannot enforce parameterized templates, approval workflows, or versioning semantics required for enterprise prompt governance. Lambda based validation also introduces operational overhead and does not replace managed prompt lifecycle controls.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to create parameterized templates, configure role based approval workflows, and enable version tracking for all prompt updates. Store final approved templates in S3 for application level consumption
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to create parameterized templates, configure role based approval workflows, and enable version tracking for all prompt updates. Store final approved templates in S3 for application level consumption Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management provides the centralized governance needed to define parameterized templates, enforce version control, and allow role specific approval workflows. This enables consistent tone, controlled updates, and collaborative prompt development across teams. Storing approved templates in S3 allows downstream services to retrieve stable, production ready prompt versions while preserving the governance process inside Prompt Management. This architecture maintains quality, accountability, and consistency across all GenAI features.

choices.text: Use DynamoDB to maintain prompt templates and use Step Functions to approve or reject updates. Track template history with CloudWatch Logs for auditability
choices.explanation: DynamoDB and Step Functions do not provide native prompt versioning, governance controls, or collaborative review workflows. CloudWatch Logs can record activities but cannot serve as a structured audit trail for managed prompt lifecycles.

questions.question: A global automotive manufacturer is developing a unified “Product Intelligence Assistant” that can help engineering, marketing, and field-service teams search across product manuals, assembly-line videos, test-drive transcripts, and CAD-annotation metadata. The assistant must support multimodal retrieval, provide streaming conversational responses with grounded citations, and route requests to different foundation models depending on the requesting business unit. Additionally, the company must strictly separate pre-release and public content and wants to avoid the operational burden of building custom multimodal ingestion pipelines or hosting models directly. What do you recommend?

choices.text: Deploy custom multimodal models on Amazon SageMaker, create a Step Functions workflow to handle text/image/video processing, and implement conditional branches for each business unit
choices.explanation: Hosting custom multimodal models in SageMaker and orchestrating steps via Step Functions significantly increases infrastructure responsibility. The requirement explicitly states minimizing hosting effort, and this approach introduces maintenance of containers and monitoring pipelines, making it unsuitable compared to Bedrock's managed orchestration.

choices.text: Use a single Anthropic Claude model in Amazon Bedrock and configure a Lambda function to classify business-unit requests. Pass all multimodal content as pre-processed text to the model and store semantic metadata inside DynamoDB
choices.explanation: This approach relies entirely on Lambda to implement multimodal routing and requires DynamoDB as a manual store for vector semantics, which is not optimized for vector search or multimodal retrieval. It also forces the organization to perform multimodal extraction outside of a managed service. This increases operational burden and does not provide native grounding or separation across pre-release and public data sources.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to ingest multimodal text, transcripts, and image-derived embeddings into separate knowledge bases for public and pre-release content. Then use Amazon Bedrock Flows to orchestrate model-specific routing based on the requesting business unit, combine the retrieval output with Titan or Claude models, and stream responses to applications through the Amazon Bedrock Converse API
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to ingest multimodal text, transcripts, and image-derived embeddings into separate knowledge bases for public and pre-release content. Then use Amazon Bedrock Flows to orchestrate model-specific routing based on the requesting business unit, combine the retrieval output with Titan or Claude models, and stream responses to applications through the Amazon Bedrock Converse API This architecture most accurately satisfies the requirement for multimodal ingestion, retrieval, routing, and response streaming with the lowest operational overhead. Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases natively handle the ingestion of mixed data formats, including long-form text, transcripts derived from videos, and extracted image features, removing the need to build custom pipelines. Because the company must keep pre-release and public data strictly separated, maintaining two independent knowledge bases is the most reliable and operationally efficient design. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html Furthermore, Amazon Bedrock Flows allow the organization to implement a fully managed orchestration layer that can route requests to separate Titan or Claude models based on department, retrieve relevant vector matches, and compose the final answer in a controlled and auditable manner. When combined with response streaming via the Amazon Bedrock Converse API, this pattern delivers low-latency conversational responses with grounded context. This solution precisely matches the company's goal to avoid custom model hosting or ingestion pipelines while achieving a robust enterprise-grade assistant. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/flows.html

choices.text: Use an Application Load Balancer with ECS services that host model adapters. Implement a custom retrieval engine using OpenSearch and a global router using API Gateway and Lambda. Enable per-unit isolation with IAM condition keys
choices.explanation: Using ECS with ALB introduces high infrastructure overhead with no direct support for retrieval, multimodal ingestion, or model orchestration. All guardrails, inputs, routing, and logging must be implemented manually, contradicting the requirement to avoid heavy custom hosting and ingestion complexity.

questions.question: A healthcare startup trains and retrains generative models on sensitive clinical notes and imaging metadata across multiple environments in different AWS accounts. Auditors now require a complete lineage graph that shows exactly which datasets, preprocessing jobs, training jobs, and model artifacts contributed to each production endpoint, including associations across accounts used for experimentation. How would you architect a lineage tracking solution that creates and connects artifacts, actions, and contexts so that auditors can reconstruct every model’s history?

choices.text: Use Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking to programmatically create artifacts for datasets, preprocessing jobs, training jobs, and model packages, and associate them through actions and contexts into a directed acyclic graph. Integrate the AddAssociation API with cross account IAM roles so that every experiment account records lineage back to the central production account endpoints
choices.explanation: Use Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking to programmatically create artifacts for datasets, preprocessing jobs, training jobs, and model packages, and associate them through actions and contexts into a directed acyclic graph. Integrate the AddAssociation API with cross account IAM roles so that every experiment account records lineage back to the central production account endpoints The solution uses Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking to model datasets, preprocessing jobs, training jobs, and model artifacts as first class lineage entities and connects them via actions and contexts into a directed acyclic graph. By integrating these API calls into training, preprocessing, and deployment workflows across accounts, the solution produces a complete, queryable lineage view that shows how every production endpoint was derived from upstream inputs. Cross account support through AddAssociation and properly scoped IAM roles allows experimentation accounts to link their artifacts to central production endpoints while maintaining security boundaries. Auditors can then query the lineage graph to reconstruct the full history of any model version, which is critical for traceability over sensitive clinical and imaging workloads. Reference: Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking Here is a deep-dive on model and data lineage in machine learning experimentation: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/use-amazon-sagemaker-model-cards-sharing-to-improve-model-governance/

choices.text: Configure AWS Glue crawlers to catalog all clinical and imaging datasets in the AWS Glue Data Catalog and rely on table relationships and job bookmarks to infer lineage across ETL and training workflows. Use Athena queries over the Data Catalog to reconstruct which datasets and jobs contributed to each model when auditors request a lineage report
choices.explanation: Using AWS Glue crawlers and the Data Catalog focuses on data discovery and schema management rather than end to end ML lineage that ties datasets to specific training jobs, model artifacts, and endpoints. While useful for cataloging data sources, Glue metadata alone cannot express the full graph of preprocessing, training, and deployment actions required to satisfy strict audit requirements for model lineage.

choices.text: Use Amazon SageMaker ML Lineage Tracking to record training jobs and model packages as artifacts in the production account, and capture dataset and preprocessing job information in tags and CloudWatch Logs. Use a separate reporting script that combines lineage APIs, tags, and logs to build lineage graphs when needed
choices.explanation: Recording only training jobs and model packages in SageMaker Lineage while leaving datasets and preprocessing jobs in tags and logs creates an incomplete lineage graph that breaks end to end traceability. Auditors would still need to piece together critical parts of the model history from unstructured sources, which defeats the goal of having a single, consistent lineage system built entirely on artifacts, actions, and contexts.

choices.text: Enable Amazon CloudWatch Logs and AWS CloudTrail for all training and endpoint invocations and export the logs into Amazon S3, then build custom Athena views that join log events to approximate lineage across preprocessing and training jobs. Rely on log timestamps and resource names to manually reconstruct model histories during audits
choices.explanation: Relying on CloudWatch Logs and CloudTrail to approximate lineage forces auditors and developers to infer relationships from timestamps and resource names, which is brittle and difficult to scale across multiple environments and accounts. Logs provide valuable observability but do not replace a structured lineage model built from artifacts, actions, and contexts that is designed for traceability and governed environments.

questions.question: A global knowledge-management platform allows users to perform diverse tasks such as contract summarization, marketing copy generation, policy rewriting, and troubleshooting support queries. Since each task demands different quality-latency-cost tradeoffs, the engineering team wants to route incoming prompts to the most suitable foundation model without requiring users to choose a model manually. The team is evaluating routing mechanisms that can intelligently inspect the prompt, classify the task category, and forward the request to the optimal LLM while maintaining a single unified interface for all use cases. Which routing strategy should the team implement?

choices.text: Implement a rules based classifier that evaluates prompt keywords and routes requests to predefined LLMs without using a model driven classification step
choices.explanation: Although this approach mimics the behavior of an LLM-assisted router, it relies entirely on static keyword matching, which cannot reliably classify complex or ambiguous prompts. LLM assisted or semantic routing approaches are preferred because they understand intent better than rule based string matching.

choices.text: Use a single general purpose LLM for all tasks and leverage prompt templates to adapt behavior
choices.explanation: A single generalist model increases cost and may degrade quality for tasks that require specialized models such as legal drafts or marketing content. This method does not implement routing and fails to optimize for task specific tradeoffs.

choices.text: Use a small generative model to rewrite incoming prompts into standardized task formats and forward all rewritten prompts to a single downstream LLM
choices.explanation: This approach resembles an LLM assisted router because it uses a lightweight model, but it does not perform true task based routing. Since all prompts are ultimately forwarded to one general purpose LLM, it does not optimize for cost, latency, or model specialization as recommended in multi LLM routing strategies.

choices.text: Implement an LLM-assisted router that uses a lightweight classifier model to examine the prompt, identify the task type, and route it to the most appropriate downstream LLM
choices.explanation: Implement an LLM-assisted router that uses a lightweight classifier model to examine the prompt, identify the task type, and route it to the most appropriate downstream LLM Multi-LLM routing strategies for generative AI applications on AWS: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/multi-llm-routing-strategies-for-generative-ai-applications-on-aws/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/multi-llm-routing-strategies-for-generative-ai-applications-on-aws/ This solution uses a small classifier LLM to inspect the prompt, determine the task category, and route the request to the best suited downstream LLM. The LLM assisted router provides flexible, context aware routing without requiring users to manually select the correct model, which improves accuracy and efficiency. By dynamically routing prompts to task specialized models, the architecture achieves better cost optimization and response quality than forcing all requests through a single generalist model. This aligns directly with the recommended routing pattern for multi LLM applications on AWS. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/multi-llm-routing-strategies-for-generative-ai-applications-on-aws/

questions.question: A multinational bank is designing a compliance-focused document review assistant for its risk division. The tool must generate grounded explanations with paragraph-level citations, enforce blocked topics using safety controls, and ensure that any new prompt template undergoes a formal approval workflow before being deployed. The bank also mandates that all model invocations be retained for ten years due to regulatory laws and that the entire system operate with minimal custom infrastructure. Which of the following represents the best solution for the given use case?

choices.text: Use SageMaker hosting with a custom LLM, implement guardrails inside the inference container, store logs in OpenSearch for 10 years, and manage prompt versioning manually via DynamoDB
choices.explanation: SageMaker custom hosting requires maintaining containers, runtimes, and model health, which contradicts the requirement for minimal infrastructure.

choices.text: Use AWS Step functions to enforce multi-stage approval workflows across business units, combine Amazon Bedrock Guardrails for topic blocking and injection resistance, and integrate Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases for retrieval with citation metadata. Then enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging with Amazon S3 Object Lock configured at 10-year governance mode retention
choices.explanation: You could certainly use AWS Step Functions for multi-stage approval workflows across business units, but it introduces additional burden of integrating with Amazon Bedrock at every stage for prompt management. In addition, you need Compliance mode (rather than Governance mode) retention to ensure a 10-year lock for the S3 objects. Governance mode allows users with special permissions to override or delete objects, while Compliance mode prevents any user, including the root account, from altering or deleting an object during its retention period.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with Titan Text models, tag documents in S3 with business-unit identifiers, and rely on CloudTrail Data Events for storing a 10-year audit trail
choices.explanation: CloudTrail Data Events do not provide the fine-grained log retention structure required for multi-year compliance on model responses and lack built-in enforcement of prompt governance or topic controls.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to enforce multi-stage approval workflows across business units, combine Amazon Bedrock Guardrails for topic blocking and injection resistance, and integrate Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases for retrieval with citation metadata. Then enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging with Amazon S3 Object Lock configured at 10-year compliance mode retention
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to enforce multi-stage approval workflows across business units, combine Amazon Bedrock Guardrails for topic blocking and injection resistance, and integrate Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases for retrieval with citation metadata. Then enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging with Amazon S3 Object Lock configured at 10-year compliance mode retention This design satisfies all of the bank’s compliance and operational needs using fully managed GenAI services. Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management allows organizations to create versioned prompt templates with mandatory approval workflows, which is essential for a regulated environment. Bedrock Guardrails prevent unsafe topics and ensure that the model’s output remains aligned with governance policies. Using Knowledge Bases ensures that all model reasoning is citation-grounded and traceable to source documents, which is an essential requirement for risk audits. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prompt-management.html Bedrock model invocation logging combined with S3 Object Lock ensures regulatory-grade retention for 10 years without building custom pipelines. With Object Lock in compliance mode, logs cannot be deleted or overwritten before expiry, satisfying legal and audit requirements. This overall architecture matches the enterprise’s preference for low operational overhead and robust governance controls while maximizing auditability, safety, traceability, and role-based workflows.

questions.question: A public sector agency uses a generative assistant to help staff draft benefit eligibility summaries based on citizen records. Policy owners are concerned about subtle bias drift over time across demographic groups and require automatic detection, alerting, and remediation when fairness metrics exceed defined thresholds. As a GenAI engineer, how would you develop a monitoring and response solution that uses explainability and bias metrics to detect biased behavior and then triggers automated actions when violations occur?

choices.text: Run daily SageMaker Processing jobs that generate bias reports and store them in Amazon S3, and have compliance analysts review the reports and manually update the model if the metrics show drift. Send email notifications using Amazon SNS whenever reports are published
choices.explanation: Generating daily bias reports through SageMaker Processing jobs shifts monitoring responsibility to manual review, which means biased behavior may persist for long periods before analysts take action. Email notifications help with awareness but do not create an automated enforcement mechanism for remediation.

choices.text: Configure SageMaker Clarify with scheduled bias drift monitoring jobs that compute fairness and explainability metrics, then publish violations to Amazon EventBridge. Use EventBridge rules to trigger automated remediation workflows in AWS Step Functions that roll back model versions or shift traffic when thresholds are crossed
choices.explanation: Configure SageMaker Clarify with scheduled bias drift monitoring jobs that compute fairness and explainability metrics, then publish violations to Amazon EventBridge. Use EventBridge rules to trigger automated remediation workflows in AWS Step Functions that roll back model versions or shift traffic when thresholds are crossed This solution uses SageMaker Clarify bias drift monitoring to calculate fairness metrics over time and identify deviations that indicate emerging disparities across demographic groups. The integration with EventBridge allows violations to be published as structured events that can immediately feed automated workflows without relying on manual review. By triggering Step Functions remediation workflows, the system can roll back model versions, shift traffic, or activate fallback logic whenever metrics exceed defined thresholds. This combination of automated detection and automated corrective action provides strong safeguards for sensitive public sector workloads that must remain compliant with fairness standards. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-model-monitor-bias-drift.html

choices.text: Enable SageMaker Clarify for pretraining bias analysis and run a weekly batch job that checks the initial bias metrics, then send the results to a DynamoDB table for record keeping. Use a Lambda function to flag significant changes in the table for any remediation steps
choices.explanation: Running only pretraining bias analysis and checking initial bias metrics fails to detect real time or ongoing bias drift, which is critical for production systems. Recording metrics in DynamoDB without integrating Clarify drift monitoring and automated workflows leaves remediation dependent on manual intervention, which does not meet the requirement for automatic detection and response.

choices.text: Set up SageMaker Clarify monitoring schedules that compute bias metrics and publish the results to Amazon EventBridge. Configure the EventBridge rules to send notifications to administrators. Allow the operations team to manually initiate remediation by updating model versions or adjusting traffic weights when alerts are received
choices.explanation: This option uses SageMaker Clarify and EventBridge correctly but does not implement automated remediation, which is a core requirement for the scenario. It relies on administrators to manually respond to bias alerts, which introduces delays and inconsistent enforcement when fairness thresholds are exceeded.

questions.question: A global retailer is building a retrieval augmented assistant that uses a foundation model over a knowledge base enriched with data from multiple SaaS systems, including CRM, ticketing, and product catalogs. The GenAI team must synchronize only the required subsets of records, handle continuous updates and deletes without performing full reloads, enforce encryption in transit and at rest, and prevent duplicate ingestion when updating the knowledge base. Which data integration solution do you recommend to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Use Amazon AppFlow to perform scheduled bulk transfers from SaaS systems and store all incoming data in Amazon S3 with server side encryption enabled. Configure AWS Glue crawlers to scan the full datasets and run transformation jobs that overwrite the knowledge base on each load
choices.explanation: This option performs bulk transfers rather than filtered or incremental loads and does not prevent duplicate ingestion. Glue crawlers and overwrite based transformations reprocess full datasets on every run and fail to meet the requirement for efficient incremental updates, delete handling, or selective synchronization.

choices.text: Use Amazon AppFlow to pull filtered subsets of SaaS data and enable field level mappings with enforced encryption in transit and at rest. Configure AWS Glue incremental jobs with job bookmarks to process only new and changed data and prevent duplicate loads into the knowledge base
choices.explanation: Use Amazon AppFlow to pull filtered subsets of SaaS data and enable field level mappings with enforced encryption in transit and at rest. Configure AWS Glue incremental jobs with job bookmarks to process only new and changed data and prevent duplicate loads into the knowledge base AppFlow supports secure, bidirectional integration with SaaS applications and allows filtering and mapping to pull only relevant subsets of data while enforcing encryption at all stages of data movement. This ensures that unnecessary records and sensitive attributes never reach the knowledge base. Glue job bookmarks track processed data and ensure that only incremental updates, including additions, modifications, and deletes, are ingested into downstream systems. This design prevents duplicate ingestion, reduces processing overhead, and keeps the knowledge base aligned with the latest SaaS changes without performing full reloads. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/appflow/latest/userguide/what-is-appflow.html

choices.text: Use AWS Glue to run full extract jobs on a fixed schedule and rely on Glue triggers to orchestrate periodic loads. Apply encryption with AWS KMS and configure a DynamoDB table to track which records have been processed
choices.explanation: Running scheduled full extract jobs increases cost and processing time and fails to meet the requirement to avoid full dataset reprocessing. Tracking processed records manually in DynamoDB adds unnecessary operational complexity and does not integrate as cleanly with SaaS source systems as AppFlow's built in incremental capabilities.

choices.text: Use Amazon S3 event notifications to detect when SaaS platforms export new files and trigger Lambda functions that write directly to the knowledge base. Rely on the knowledge base to deduplicate records and ignore outdated entries
choices.explanation: Relying on SaaS exports and S3 event notifications does not provide fine grained filtering or selective synchronization, and offloading deduplication to the knowledge base risks inconsistent data quality. This approach also lacks a mechanism to capture deletes and incremental updates in real time.

questions.question: A SaaS provider is developing a conversational assistant that can answer customer questions by referencing proprietary implementation guides, internal processes, and highly customized runbooks. Each customer’s documents must stay fully isolated from other tenants so that no organization ever gains visibility into another’s intellectual property. The legal team also requires strict controls to ensure that none of the customer provided documents are used to train or improve the underlying language models. The architecture must allow the assistant to generate grounded responses while maintaining strong privacy guarantees for every customer. As a GenAI developer, which solution would you recommend?

choices.text: Provide each customer’s documents directly to the model at runtime and control exposure by encrypting the files with a customer specific KMS key. Allow the foundation model to generate grounded answers by reading unprocessed documents and leverage output postprocessing to ensure private data is not leaked
choices.explanation: Providing raw customer documents directly to the model bypasses the structured safeguards of knowledge bases and increases the risk of accidental exposure. Encryption alone does not prevent unintended use of private content once decrypted at inference time, and output filtering cannot guarantee complete protection of sensitive material.

choices.text: Create a separate vector index per customer inside Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases but allow the GenAI backend to share a common retrieval role that can access all indexes. Depend on the application layer to filter retrieved content by customer ID before passing context to the model
choices.explanation: Even if each customer has a separate vector index, using a shared retrieval role introduces a cross tenant access path that violates isolation requirements. Application layer filtering is insufficient because the retrieval call itself may already expose embeddings or documents from other customers before the filter is applied.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to store and index each customer’s documents in isolated data stores and configure resource based policies so that only that customer’s assistant can retrieve embeddings and context. Leverage Amazon Bedrock’s native privacy guarantees, which ensure that none of the customer provided data is used to train or improve foundation models, while grounding responses exclusively from the customer’s isolated knowledge base
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to store and index each customer’s documents in isolated data stores and configure resource based policies so that only that customer’s assistant can retrieve embeddings and context. Leverage Amazon Bedrock’s native privacy guarantees, which ensure that none of the customer provided data is used to train or improve foundation models, while grounding responses exclusively from the customer’s isolated knowledge base Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases support strict isolation through per resource access controls and separate storage locations for embeddings and source content. Using separate knowledge bases for each customer ensures that retrieval never crosses tenant boundaries, while resource based policies and IAM constraints prevent any unauthorized access to another entity’s data. Amazon Bedrock also guarantees that customer provided documents are never used to train or improve the underlying foundation models. By grounding responses solely from each customer’s isolated knowledge base, the provider can deliver personalized and secure assistance without violating legal or privacy requirements. Here is a deep-dive on access control for vector stores using metadata filtering with Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/access-control-for-vector-stores-using-metadata-filtering-with-knowledge-bases-for-amazon-bedrock/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/access-control-for-vector-stores-using-metadata-filtering-with-knowledge-bases-for-amazon-bedrock/

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to store and index customer documents in separate logical indexes while allowing all indexes to share a single underlying storage location and unified access policy for simplified management. Leverage Amazon Bedrock’s privacy guarantees to ensure that customer data is not used to train models and depend on application side routing to direct retrieval queries to the correct customer context
choices.explanation: Sharing a common storage layer and unified access policy undermines the required tenant level isolation because a single misconfiguration or retrieval request can expose content across customers. Application side routing cannot enforce strong access boundaries, and retrieval level separation must be enforced at the resource and policy layers within Bedrock Knowledge Bases to prevent cross tenant leakage.

questions.question: A generative AI application retrieves documents from an internal knowledge repository to supply context to a foundation model before generating responses. The repository contains a mix of unstructured text, structured attributes such as document classification and ownership, and technical terminology that varies across teams. Users often phrase queries differently even when referring to the same concepts. During evaluation, the team observes inconsistent retrieval behavior across different query types. Some searches return documents that are conceptually related but not operationally appropriate, while other searches fail to retrieve useful context when exact terminology is not used. The team wants to redesign the retrieval layer to improve overall relevance and consistency of results passed to the model. Which option is the MOST effective way to design the retrieval solution?

choices.text: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service to run vector similarity queries and keyword searches as separate operations. Merge and de duplicate the two result sets in the application layer before selecting documents for the model. Tune each retrieval method independently to improve overall coverage
choices.explanation: Vector similarity alone does not account for structured constraints such as document classification or ownership. Increasing the number of retrieved results raises recall but also increases noise, which can negatively impact model grounding. Filtering after retrieval does not correct ranking inaccuracies introduced earlier. Shifting relevance decisions to application code adds complexity and reduces the effectiveness of search engine scoring. This approach can lead to inconsistent behavior as query patterns change.

choices.text: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service to index documents with both text fields and vector embeddings, and execute a single query that evaluates similarity and structured attributes together. Combine vector similarity scoring with keyword matching and metadata filters inside the search engine. Adjust scoring weights to balance semantic relevance and exact matches
choices.explanation: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service to index documents with both text fields and vector embeddings, and execute a single query that evaluates similarity and structured attributes together. Combine vector similarity scoring with keyword matching and metadata filters inside the search engine. Adjust scoring weights to balance semantic relevance and exact matches This approach evaluates semantic similarity and structured constraints within the same query execution. By allowing the search engine to consider both vector relevance and keyword or metadata matches at once, ranking remains consistent and reflects multiple relevance signals. This produces more balanced results across diverse query styles. Running hybrid evaluation inside Amazon OpenSearch Service enables efficient scoring and ranking without shifting responsibility to the application layer. Weighting allows fine tuning how much influence each signal has, which helps handle both conceptual similarity and strict operational requirements. This improves the quality of context provided to the foundation model. Designing retrieval this way reduces missed results and limits the inclusion of unsuitable documents. It provides a robust foundation for retrieval augmented generation workflows operating on complex enterprise datasets. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/hybrid-search-with-amazon-opensearch-service/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/hybrid-search-with-amazon-opensearch-service/

choices.text: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service to prioritize keyword and metadata search for all queries and fall back to vector similarity when no results are returned. Enforce strict metadata filtering to maintain consistency across searches. Avoid blending relevance signals to simplify ranking behavior
choices.explanation: Vector similarity alone does not account for structured constraints such as document classification or ownership. Increasing the number of retrieved results raises recall but also increases noise, which can negatively impact model grounding. Filtering after retrieval does not correct ranking inaccuracies introduced earlier. Shifting relevance decisions to application code adds complexity and reduces the effectiveness of search engine scoring. This approach can lead to inconsistent behavior as query patterns change.

choices.text: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service to index document embeddings and execute vector similarity queries for all searches. Increase the number of retrieved results to compensate for variation in phrasing. Leverage post retrieval filtering in application logic to remove unsuitable documents
choices.explanation: Vector similarity alone does not account for structured constraints such as document classification or ownership. Increasing the number of retrieved results raises recall but also increases noise, which can negatively impact model grounding. Filtering after retrieval does not correct ranking inaccuracies introduced earlier. Shifting relevance decisions to application code adds complexity and reduces the effectiveness of search engine scoring. This approach can lead to inconsistent behavior as query patterns change.

questions.question: A research group uses a GenAI assistant to answer questions over large technical reports that frequently exceed the context limits of the chosen foundation model. The current design forwards entire report sections into the prompt, which drives up token usage, increases latency, and often truncates important contextual clauses. The group wants to redesign the context assembly and prompt construction so that the system optimizes context window usage through pruning and compression while preserving the most relevant content? What do you recommend?

choices.text: Adopt Amazon Comprehend to classify document sections and send all sections belonging to matching categories directly to Amazon Bedrock. Avoid additional chunking logic because category level grouping ensures that the model receives broad thematic context
choices.explanation: Classifying document sections and forwarding all category matched content does not solve the context limit problem and still sends large amounts of text to the model.
