Category based grouping is not a substitute for granular retrieval or compression and will continue to inflate token usage. This approach offers minimal improvements in efficiency or precision.

choices.text: Implement a retrieval pipeline that ranks passages using Amazon OpenSearch Serverless, then apply prompt compression techniques to summarize selected passages before injecting them into Amazon Bedrock for final inference. Use dynamic context pruning so only the highest relevance spans enter the final prompt
choices.explanation: Implement a retrieval pipeline that ranks passages using Amazon OpenSearch Serverless, then apply prompt compression techniques to summarize selected passages before injecting them into Amazon Bedrock for final inference. Use dynamic context pruning so only the highest relevance spans enter the final prompt The most effective approach is to retrieve only the most relevant document spans and reduce their token footprint using structured summarization or prompt compression. This lowers the total tokens entering the prompt, reduces latency, and ensures that only high value content reaches the model. Combining retrieval ranking with compression preserves semantic fidelity without exceeding the model's context limit. Dynamic pruning ensures that the prompt adapts to the query and context load in real time, which avoids unnecessary tokens and prevents truncation of important clauses. This approach maximizes both quality and efficiency, especially for workloads involving large technical documents and strict context window limits.

choices.text: Use Amazon Textract to extract all text from the documents and store complete sections in Amazon S3. Feed these full sections into Amazon Bedrock by increasing the model's maximum token setting and rely on the model to handle truncation efficiently
choices.explanation: Increasing the model's input token allowance is not feasible because foundation models have fixed context windows. Passing unfiltered full sections leads to high latency, unnecessary token costs, and a high risk of truncation. This approach results in lower relevance and poor performance.

choices.text: Implement a retrieval pipeline with Amazon OpenSearch Serverless and forward all retrieved passages directly to Amazon Bedrock without applying any summarization or compression. Use a static relevance threshold so that all passages above the threshold are always included in the prompt for broader context coverage
choices.explanation: Relying only on retrieval and passing all matched spans without compression does not control token growth or prevent context overflow. A static threshold cannot adapt to different query lengths or content density, which leads to excessive tokens, higher latency, and a greater chance of prompt truncation. This approach fails to optimize context window usage because it does not reduce text size before inference.

questions.question: A media company wants to add a conversational assistant into its existing customer portal serving both web and mobile clients. The solution must return real-time responses, support GraphQL queries that fetch and update domain data alongside foundation model outputs, and convert incoming GraphQL requests into structured prompts for the foundation model while keeping latency low and hiding model API details from client applications. Which solution would you implement to address these requirements?

choices.text: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate GraphQL parsing and foundation model calls and return results through Amazon EventBridge. Integrate the customer portal with EventBridge to fetch conversational responses from subscribed event targets
choices.explanation: Step Functions is designed for long running or orchestrated workflows and cannot deliver the low latency, synchronous response model required for conversational assistants. EventBridge is also not suitable for direct client integration because it lacks native mechanisms for request response flows or real-time communication with user applications.

choices.text: Use AWS AppSync with pipeline resolvers that invoke a Velocity Template Language (VTL) template to forward GraphQL requests directly to the foundation model endpoint. Rely on AppSync caching to reduce latency and expose the model output to clients without using Lambda for prompt transformation
choices.explanation: This option forwards GraphQL requests directly to the model endpoint, which does not allow the required prompt transformation, input shaping, or structured orchestration that Lambda resolvers provide. AppSync caching is not suitable for dynamic conversational outputs because each request produces unique responses, and bypassing Lambda prevents centralizing model integration logic.

choices.text: Use AWS AppSync with direct Lambda resolvers to process GraphQL queries and transform them into foundation model prompts. Implement the inference logic inside the Lambda function and return structured outputs to clients while keeping model details hidden
choices.explanation: Use AWS AppSync with direct Lambda resolvers to process GraphQL queries and transform them into foundation model prompts. Implement the inference logic inside the Lambda function and return structured outputs to clients while keeping model details hidden AppSync provides a managed GraphQL service that can combine domain data access and foundation model inference within a single API while maintaining low latency. Direct Lambda resolvers allow the system to transform GraphQL requests into structured prompts, invoke the model, and merge the results with application data in real time. This approach keeps client applications unaware of the underlying model APIs, centralizes prompt transformation logic, and supports both web and mobile clients through a unified GraphQL interface. AppSync also provides efficient data fetching and caching features that further reduce latency and round trips.

choices.text: Use an API Gateway HTTP API fronting a Lambda function that parses GraphQL payloads and invokes the foundation model endpoint. Cache responses in API Gateway to reduce model invocations and expose the HTTP API directly to web and mobile clients
choices.explanation: API Gateway HTTP APIs do not provide built-in GraphQL support or resolver level data fetching and therefore require custom parsing and stitching logic that increases latency and complexity. Caching GraphQL responses is unreliable because conversational outputs and domain data often change per request, and exposing HTTP APIs directly to clients does not offer the unified GraphQL interface required.

questions.question: A global healthcare analytics company operates a clinical summarization system that currently runs in a single geographic region. New regulatory and availability requirements mandate that the system must remain fully functional even if one region experiences a prolonged outage, while also ensuring that user requests are routed according to regional compliance rules. You are responsible for designing a multi Region architecture that supports active active or active passive deployments, performs automated health checks, and redirects traffic safely during failures. How should you architect the multi Region integration and routing strategy to meet these resilience requirements?

choices.text: Run the Bedrock workload in one AWS Region and enable cross Region replication for data, while continuing to direct all inference requests through a single Route 53 simple record. Allow DNS caching to manage failover behavior without using health checks or routing policies
choices.explanation: Running inference in only one Region with simple DNS routing prevents traffic from shifting away from the failed Region and does not satisfy multi Region resilience needs. Replicating data does not enable automatic failover when the inference endpoint becomes unavailable.

choices.text: Deploy the Bedrock based inference workflow in two AWS Regions and expose each through separate regional endpoints registered in Amazon Route 53 using failover or latency based routing. Configure Route 53 health checks to monitor each endpoint and automatically redirect traffic to the healthy Region during outages
choices.explanation: Deploy the Bedrock based inference workflow in two AWS Regions and expose each through separate regional endpoints registered in Amazon Route 53 using failover or latency based routing. Configure Route 53 health checks to monitor each endpoint and automatically redirect traffic to the healthy Region during outages Deploying regional Bedrock endpoints behind Route 53 failover or latency based routing provides a robust multi Region pattern that supports regulatory compliance and uninterrupted clinical access. Route 53 health checks continuously monitor endpoint health so that requests seamlessly shift to the healthy Region during prolonged outages. This approach ensures predictable routing behavior, automatic failover, and geographic control while maintaining low latency and high availability. It meets both the regulatory requirement for region specific routing and the operational need for business continuity.

choices.text: Create separate Bedrock endpoints in multiple AWS Regions but configure Route 53 with weighted routing that always favors the primary Region regardless of health status. Allow traffic to shift back only after administrators manually update routing weights following an outage.
choices.explanation: Weighted routing that always prefers a primary Region cannot detect outages and will continue sending traffic to an unhealthy endpoint until weights are manually updated. This approach does not fulfill the requirement for automated failover or region specific routing controls.

choices.text: Deploy the Bedrock workflow in two AWS Regions but front both services with a single Route 53 record that uses simple routing and has no health checks enabled. Leverage application clients to detect endpoint failures and manually shift requests to the secondary Region
choices.explanation: A single Route 53 record without routing policies or health checks cannot provide automated failover across Regions, forcing clients to handle failure detection themselves. This design violates continuity requirements and introduces unpredictable behavior during regional outages.

questions.question: A healthcare provider is exposing internal foundation model endpoints to clinicians through several existing web applications that already authenticate users against an enterprise identity provider. The security team wants to federate identities into AWS, enrich tokens with department and role information, enforce least privilege access to foundation model APIs based on user attributes and request context such as time of day and source network, and centralize authorization logic without modifying the existing login flows. As a GenAI engineer, which approach should you implement to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Use Amazon Cognito user pools as the primary identity provider for all applications and migrate clinician identities from the enterprise provider. Apply resource based policies on the foundation model endpoints and enforce access controls through security group rules
choices.explanation: Migrating clinicians into a new Cognito user pool breaks the requirement of preserving existing authentication flows and does not provide the SAML federation needed to use enterprise issued identities. Resource based policies and security groups do not offer adequate fine grained authorization to classify model access by department, context, or time of day.

choices.text: Use Amazon Cognito with SAML federation to the enterprise identity provider and configure pre token generation Lambda triggers to enrich tokens with department and role attributes. Apply IAM policies with condition keys for source IP and time based controls to enforce least privilege access to foundation model APIs
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Cognito with SAML federation to the enterprise identity provider and configure pre token generation Lambda triggers to enrich tokens with department and role attributes. Apply IAM policies with condition keys for source IP and time based controls to enforce least privilege access to foundation model APIs Cognito with SAML federation integrates seamlessly with enterprise identity providers and allows applications to keep their existing authentication flows while still federating identities into AWS. The pre token generation Lambda trigger allows the system to enrich ID or access tokens with trusted department and role metadata before they are used to authorize access. IAM policies with context based conditions, such as source IP, department attributes, or time of day, provide strong least privilege control over foundation model API access. This design centralizes authorization decisions and avoids the need to rewrite the legacy application authentication layer. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/cognito-user-pools-saml-idp.html

choices.text: Use IAM Identity Center with automatic user provisioning and map enterprise roles directly to IAM roles for foundation model access. Enforce time based and network based restrictions using service control policies applied to the clinician organizational units
choices.explanation: IAM Identity Center is not the right fit when existing applications already handle authentication through an external identity provider and cannot be rewritten, and service control policies cannot enforce fine grained, context aware authorization for individual foundation model API calls. This approach does not offer token enrichment capabilities needed for clinician department and role mapping.

choices.text: Use API Gateway custom authorizers to validate tokens issued by the enterprise identity provider and attach model access permissions directly to each API method. Manage clinician attributes in DynamoDB tables and perform attribute checks inside Lambda authorizers
choices.explanation: API Gateway custom authorizers require additional complexity and do not integrate natively with enterprise federation flows or token enrichment needs. Storing and validating attributes manually in DynamoDB introduces operational overhead and does not satisfy centralized, policy based least privilege enforcement across the foundation model APIs.

questions.question: A regulatory compliance team at a financial services company must ensure that its GenAI safety pipeline remains resilient against evolving adversarial inputs. The team wants to run periodic stress tests that inject synthetic jailbreak prompts, policy evasion attempts, manipulation phrases, and role overriding instructions into the system to verify that all safety layers remain effective. They also want the workflow to execute on a set schedule, automatically evaluate outputs for policy violations, and generate alerts or reports whenever a failure is detected. Which of the following options would you recommend for the given scenario?

choices.text: Use Amazon SageMaker to train a small classifier that predicts whether a model output is safe, then manually upload adversarial test prompts into an S3 bucket when tests are needed. Trigger a Lambda function that processes the prompts and emails the results to the compliance team
choices.explanation: Manually uploading test prompts to S3 introduces operational overhead and prevents consistent scheduled testing. A SageMaker classifier can help label data but does not provide a full automation pipeline for generating adversarial prompts, executing tests, and reporting failures in real time.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to run scheduled safety tests automatically and leverage CloudWatch alarms to detect when test prompts produce unsafe responses. Add a Lambda function that logs the results to Amazon S3 for later review
choices.explanation: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails does not provide built in scheduled testing or automated adversarial injection capabilities, so it cannot drive periodic stress testing by itself. CloudWatch alarms can detect unsafe responses after they occur but do not orchestrate end to end synthetic adversarial workflows.

choices.text: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate scheduled adversarial test runs, invoke the model with synthetic attack prompts, and route outputs through a Lambda based validation layer that checks for safety violations. Configure CloudWatch Events to trigger the workflow on a recurring schedule and publish alerts when failures occur
choices.explanation: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate scheduled adversarial test runs, invoke the model with synthetic attack prompts, and route outputs through a Lambda based validation layer that checks for safety violations. Configure CloudWatch Events to trigger the workflow on a recurring schedule and publish alerts when failures occur AWS Step Functions is the ideal choice for orchestrating a multi step adversarial testing pipeline because it supports coordinated execution of synthetic test prompts, validation stages, and conditional branching based on failures. By combining Step Functions with a Lambda based evaluator, the system can automatically analyze model outputs for safety violations and apply custom detection logic suited to regulatory requirements. Using CloudWatch Events to schedule recurring executions ensures that adversarial testing runs without manual intervention and maintains regular coverage of evolving risks. Integrating alerting based on detected failures provides fast feedback loops that help compliance teams address vulnerabilities before they reach production environments.

choices.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to classify adversarial intent in synthetic prompts and customize the model to refuse unsafe requests automatically. Store test outputs in DynamoDB and review them during quarterly compliance audits
choices.explanation: Amazon Comprehend cannot reliably detect adversarial manipulation strategies because jailbreak and prompt injection patterns require specialized logic. Storing results in DynamoDB for quarterly review does not meet the requirement for automated alerts or scheduled evaluations.

questions.question: A financial research platform wants to modernize how analysts and internal teams search through a large corpus of over 100,000 research reports. These documents span multiple sectors and sub-sectors and are frequently updated as market conditions change. The company wants a fully managed solution that automatically chunks documents, generates embeddings, organizes content in a hierarchical manner, and provides semantic retrieval capabilities without requiring the team to manage vector indexes, clusters, or infrastructure. Which architecture should a GenAI developer implement to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Configure an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base to automatically chunk source documents, generate embeddings, and store vectors in a fully managed vector index. Use hierarchical metadata and source-table configuration to organize documents across sectors and sub-sectors
choices.explanation: Configure an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base to automatically chunk source documents, generate embeddings, and store vectors in a fully managed vector index. Use hierarchical metadata and source-table configuration to organize documents across sectors and sub-sectors Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases is a fully managed retrieval augmentation service that handles automatic document chunking, embedding generation, and vector index storage without requiring customers to manage any vector infrastructure. The service also supports hierarchical document organization through metadata, which is crucial for a financial research platform that categorizes reports by sectors and sub-sectors. This option meets all requirements because it offers serverless scaling, hands-free vector index management, and native integration with foundation models for high quality semantic retrieval. It allows teams to ingest documents, define metadata, and retrieve information using managed retrieval APIs without maintaining clusters or external vector engines. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html

choices.text: Deploy Amazon OpenSearch Service with the Neural Search plugin for semantic retrieval. Configure the Neural Search plugin to store-embeddings and enable hybrid search across the financial documents. Build custom pipelines to perform document chunking and maintain vector indexes as the content grows
choices.explanation: This option supports semantic and hybrid search but requires the team to manage OpenSearch indexes, configure sharding, maintain infrastructure, and build custom pipelines for chunking and embedding. Because the question explicitly requires a fully managed solution with automatic chunking and no vector storage maintenance, this approach does not satisfy the operational requirements.

choices.text: Store structured metadata in Amazon RDS PostgreSQL with pgvector extension and keep the full reports in Amazon S3. Implement custom ETL pipelines to chunk documents and maintain vector embeddings inside the RDS database
choices.explanation: This architecture allows combining relational metadata and document storage, but it shifts responsibility for chunking, embedding generation, and vector index maintenance to the development team. Since the question clearly states that the company wants to avoid managing vector infrastructure, RDS pgvector is not a suitable choice.

choices.text: Configure AWS Glue to preprocess and chunk documents before storing them in Amazon S3 and run Athena queries on enriched metadata tables to filter relevant documents. Build a separate custom embedding generation workflow and store vectors in an external vector index managed by the development team
choices.explanation: This approach relies heavily on custom orchestration and requires the team to manage embedding workflows, vector indexing, and retrieval logic, which violates the requirement to avoid managing vector infrastructure. While Glue and Athena can assist with ETL and metadata filtering, they do not provide managed vector storage, hierarchical organization, or automatic embedding generation.

questions.question: A research laboratory is working with an advanced open source language model that is extremely large and cannot fit into the memory of a single accelerator, even when using the most powerful hardware available. The team wants to make this model accessible to internal researchers for prototyping and interactive experimentation, but reducing its size would remove essential reasoning and comprehension abilities that the group depends on. How would you design the inference architecture using distributed loading techniques and multi-instance compute configurations so that the model can run reliably and handle user requests?

choices.text: Use SageMaker AI real time inference with UltraServers style multi instance configurations and rely on data parallelism to distribute the workload across GPUs. Configure each instance to run a full copy of the model and use synchronized execution to coordinate responses
choices.explanation: Loading identical full copies of a multi hundred gigabyte model on each instance does not solve the memory problem and will cause startup failure or GPU exhaustion. Without model parallelism, UltraServers configurations cannot divide the model across devices, making this approach infeasible for oversized LLMs.

choices.text: Host the model on a single large GPU instance and increase GPU memory allocation through container settings to overcome model size limitations. Leverage default container initialization values to complete the loading process
choices.explanation: A single GPU instance cannot accommodate a model that exceeds the memory capacity of the largest available accelerator, and increasing container memory limits cannot expand physical GPU memory. Default initialization behavior will cause repeated failures because the model cannot load into a single device.

choices.text: Use SageMaker AI real time inference with UltraServers style multi instance configurations and enable model parallelism libraries such as DeepSpeed or FasterTransformer to shard the model across multiple GPUs. Configure the endpoint to load coordinated model partitions across instances and use high performance networking to support cross device communication during inference
choices.explanation: Use SageMaker AI real time inference with UltraServers style multi instance configurations and enable model parallelism libraries such as DeepSpeed or FasterTransformer to shard the model across multiple GPUs. Configure the endpoint to load coordinated model partitions across instances and use high performance networking to support cross device communication during inference via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/deploy-large-models-at-high-performance-using-fastertransformer-on-amazon-sagemaker/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/deploy-large-models-at-high-performance-using-fastertransformer-on-amazon-sagemaker/ Model parallelism is the primary technique for running extremely large models that cannot fit into the memory of a single device. Libraries such as DeepSpeed or FasterTransformer enable sharding the model across multiple GPUs and coordinate execution by distributing layers or tensor blocks across nodes. UltraServers style multi instance configurations provide the high bandwidth networking that allows these components to communicate efficiently during inference. This approach allows the model to operate at full size without modification and supports reliable serving of inference requests. Distributed partitioning ensures that each component of the model resides on the correct hardware while maintaining low latency cross device communication. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/deploy-large-models-at-high-performance-using-fastertransformer-on-amazon-sagemaker/

choices.text: Use SageMaker AI real time inference with UltraServers style multi instance configurations but disable model parallelism so that each instance loads identical full copies of the model. Increase instance count and rely on horizontal scaling to handle memory limitations
choices.explanation: Loading identical full copies of a multi hundred gigabyte model on each instance does not solve the memory problem and will cause startup failure or GPU exhaustion. Without model parallelism, UltraServers configurations cannot divide the model across devices, making this approach infeasible for oversized LLMs.

questions.question: A healthcare information portal is rolling out a GenAI assistant for clinicians that summarizes guidelines and suggests differential diagnoses. Clinicians report that the assistant provides final answers without revealing the reasoning behind them, and they want visibility into intermediate steps, confidence indicators, and the sequence of decisions taken as the response is generated. As the GenAI experience owner, how would you redesign the interaction pattern and user interface to make the assistant's decision process more transparent?

choices.text: Create a Lambda function that post processes model outputs to add generic disclaimers and a static confidence score before sending results to the UI. Store these generated summaries in CloudWatch Logs for later transparency reviews
choices.explanation: Post processing outputs with Lambda introduces generic disclaimers but does not reflect the model's actual reasoning process. Storing static summaries in CloudWatch Logs does not help clinicians interpret the model's stepwise decision making.

choices.text: Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to block incomplete or low confidence outputs and show only fully validated responses to clinicians. Use CloudWatch alarms to notify administrators whenever reasoning steps are filtered during generation
choices.explanation: Guardrails focus on content safety and filtering rather than exposing reasoning or confidence details. Blocking partial reasoning steps prevents clinicians from understanding the logic behind diagnostic suggestions and reduces transparency.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock streaming to deliver intermediate reasoning steps as they are generated and render these steps in the UI through a structured reasoning display component. Configure structured output prompts in Prompt Flows that extract reasoning chains and confidence indicators so clinicians can see how each conclusion is formed
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock streaming to deliver intermediate reasoning steps as they are generated and render these steps in the UI through a structured reasoning display component. Configure structured output prompts in Prompt Flows that extract reasoning chains and confidence indicators so clinicians can see how each conclusion is formed Streaming intermediate reasoning and structured explanations gives clinicians real time visibility into the steps taken by the model. Using Prompt Flows with structured outputs allows transparent extraction of reasoning chains and confidence indicators, which supports higher trust and better interpretability in medical decision workflows. This approach aligns direct model reasoning with user facing UI components, enabling clinicians to understand how each diagnostic suggestion was formed. Combining streaming with structured reasoning output ensures that transparency remains consistent across different model responses.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock streaming to deliver the model's full response in smaller chunks and present it incrementally in the UI. Leverage CloudWatch metrics to track confidence levels in the background instead of exposing step by step reasoning directly to clinicians
choices.explanation: Streaming partial chunks of a final response does not expose true intermediate reasoning or structured confidence indicators. Background metric tracking cannot replace explicit reasoning display, which clinicians require for transparency in diagnostic support tools.

questions.question: A global retailer is developing a GenAI powered product assistant that must answer questions about product specifications, warranty rules, compatibility details, and regulatory standards drawn from thousands of internal documents. Early testing shows that the model often fabricates details that do not appear in the authorized documentation, which results in inaccurate answers being shown to customers. The engineering leadership wants every response generated by the assistant to be grounded in verified enterprise documents, with a mechanism that checks whether the final output truly aligns with trusted information before returning it to the user. What do you suggest?

choices.text: Use Amazon Kendra as the primary retrieval layer and allow the LLM to incorporate retrieved passages into a free form response without enforcing alignment. Rely on CloudWatch alerts to detect when the model produces hallucinated details
choices.explanation: Using Amazon Kendra without enforcing grounding allows the model to generate free form answers that may still include hallucinated details. Relying on CloudWatch alerts to detect hallucinations is reactive and does not prevent inaccurate information from being shown to customers.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to index documents but allow the model to respond directly without retrieval based grounding. Apply a post processing Lambda function that logs fabricated answers for later review
choices.explanation: Indexing documents in a knowledge base but skipping retrieval based grounding allows the model to produce unsupported answers. Logging fabricated outputs for later review does not prevent hallucinations from appearing in real time.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to retrieve authoritative product data and ground model responses, then apply confidence scoring and semantic similarity checks to verify alignment before returning the final answer. Configure the system to return only evidence backed responses when grounding confidence is high
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to retrieve authoritative product data and ground model responses, then apply confidence scoring and semantic similarity checks to verify alignment before returning the final answer. Configure the system to return only evidence backed responses when grounding confidence is high Grounding responses through Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases ensures that the model retrieves and uses only validated enterprise documents as its source of truth. Confidence scoring and semantic similarity checks help verify that the model output aligns with the retrieved passages, which significantly reduces the risk of hallucinations. Combining retrieval based grounding with verification ensures that only evidence supported responses reach the customer. This approach directly aligns with best practices for building accuracy verification systems in GenAI applications. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails-contextual-grounding-check.html via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails-contextual-grounding-check.html

choices.text: Use Amazon Translate followed by a summarization model to generate simplified versions of the product documents and instruct the model to answer only from those summaries. Depend on model guidelines and prompt instructions to reduce hallucinations
choices.explanation: Translating and summarizing documents before feeding them to the model removes detailed context required for accurate grounding. Relying on prompt instructions alone is insufficient to prevent hallucinations in enterprise environments.

questions.question: An analytics organization has accumulated years of customer feedback distributed across hundreds of storage locations, with no clear inventory of which files contain sensitive details such as personal identifiers, financial information, or regulated content. The GenAI development team wants to build both fine tuned models and retrieval pipelines from this data, but the security team insists on gaining automated visibility into the presence of sensitive information before any dataset is used for GenAI processing. Leadership also wants a comprehensive method to categorize, protect, and track risky files so that only approved datasets flow into AI workflows. What do you recommend for the given use case?

choices.text: Use Amazon Athena to query sample objects from S3 and rely on pattern matching queries to estimate which files might contain regulated fields. Push all candidate datasets to a staging bucket and use IAM conditions to control downstream access during model training
choices.explanation: Sampling data through Athena queries cannot reliably detect all sensitive content and does not provide continuous monitoring across all objects in S3. IAM rules cannot identify or classify PII, and pushing datasets to staging buckets may still expose regulated data to model training workflows.

choices.text: Enable Amazon Macie with default monitoring. Leverage the automated object sampling to identify sensitive files in the S3 environment efficiently. Use the partial findings to label only the detected objects and allow the remaining unscanned data to flow into fine tuning and retrieval pipelines.
choices.explanation: Using only default Macie monitoring without configuring full discovery jobs limits coverage to sampled or incidental detections and misses many sensitive objects across large or distributed S3 datasets. Allowing unscanned data to enter model training or RAG pipelines creates significant compliance gaps because sensitive content can flow into AI workloads without being classified or protected.

choices.text: Configure Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to block sensitive categories and rely on the guardrail rules to detect and prevent PII from entering fine tuning or retrieval datasets stored in S3. Allow the RAG pipeline to ingest all S3 objects and depend on the guardrail filtering layer to ensure that only compliant data is surfaced to the model
choices.explanation: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails operate at model input and output time and cannot scan or classify objects stored in S3 before they enter training or retrieval workflows. Guardrails do not replace Macie for data at rest discovery and would allow sensitive objects to reach downstream stages without proper identification, classification, or protection.

choices.text: Enable Amazon Macie across the relevant S3 buckets and configure automated sensitive data discovery jobs for full discovery to scan objects for PII and regulated patterns. Use the classification findings to tag, quarantine, or restrict access to high risk objects before allowing them into fine tuning or RAG workflows
choices.explanation: Enable Amazon Macie across the relevant S3 buckets and configure automated sensitive data discovery jobs for full discovery to scan objects for PII and regulated patterns. Use the classification findings to tag, quarantine, or restrict access to high risk objects before allowing them into fine tuning or RAG workflows Amazon Macie provides automated discovery of sensitive content across S3 buckets using managed detectors that identify a broad set of personal and regulated data types. Running classification jobs across all relevant storage locations ensures the team has a complete and accurate inventory of high risk objects before they enter any AI pipeline. Using Macie findings enables the analytics and security teams to quarantine sensitive data, apply restrictive access controls, or tag datasets for approved workflows. This approach establishes a repeatable, automated governance layer that prevents unsafe datasets from reaching fine tuned models or retrieval indexes. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd.html How automated sensitive data discovery works: via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html

questions.question: A financial institution operates in several countries that require local processing of sensitive customer data, while central analytics and shared services run in an AWS Region. The GenAI platform team wants to deploy foundation model inference close to on-premises data for compliance, reduce latency for users in major metropolitan areas, and connect on-premises, edge, and regional environments using a unified routing and security design that respects strict data residency rules. Which architecture should you implement to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Deploy AWS Snowball Edge devices in each country to run inference workloads and periodically sync output to the central Region. Use internet based VPN connections to link metropolitan users with the nearest Snowball Edge location
choices.explanation: Snowball Edge devices are not designed for continuous enterprise inference workloads that require persistent hybrid connectivity and are better suited for temporary, disconnected, or migration use cases. Relying on internet based VPNs to connect users to these devices introduces latency, lower reliability, and does not meet regulated data residency or availability requirements.

choices.text: Use Local Zones in all countries for foundation model inference and connect on-premises systems to the Region through site-to-site VPN tunnels. Add AWS Global Accelerator for traffic distribution and rely on routing policies to enforce data residency requirements
choices.explanation: Local Zones do not provide data residency guarantees and are not intended for running compliance bound workloads that require local processing of sensitive information. Global Accelerator cannot enforce jurisdiction specific residency restrictions, and VPN connectivity lacks the deterministic, high bandwidth characteristics needed for hybrid GenAI workloads.

choices.text: Place all foundation model inference workloads in a single central Region and use AWS Wavelength Zones to serve metropolitan users with lower latency. Backhaul all on-premises data to the Region through Direct Connect gateways for centralized processing and storage
choices.explanation: Wavelength Zones are optimized for mobile edge computing and do not serve as a data residency solution or a replacement for local computation of sensitive workloads. Centralizing all inference in a single Region violates country level residency requirements and creates unnecessary latency for both on-premises systems and metro area users.

choices.text: Deploy AWS Outposts racks in each country to run foundation model inference locally and use AWS Direct Connect with Transit Gateway to integrate on-premises networks with the central Region. Configure Local Zones in metro areas that require low latency and enforce residency controls using region specific routing domains
choices.explanation: Deploy AWS Outposts racks in each country to run foundation model inference locally and use AWS Direct Connect with Transit Gateway to integrate on-premises networks with the central Region. Configure Local Zones in metro areas that require low latency and enforce residency controls using region specific routing domains This approach satisfies strict data residency requirements by using AWS Outposts racks, which allow foundation model inference to run physically within each country while maintaining operational consistency with the Region. Local Zones provide low latency access in dense metropolitan areas, and Transit Gateway with Direct Connect creates a controlled hybrid network design that supports predictable routing and security boundaries. The architecture aligns with enterprise hybrid design patterns because it allows local processing of sensitive data, avoids cross border data movement, and integrates monitoring, security controls, and IAM patterns consistently across all environments. Dedicated connectivity and regional isolation mechanisms ensure that both performance and compliance needs are met.

questions.question: A scientific research organization is building an advanced GenAI workspace that allows researchers to perform two very different categories of computations. Some tasks involve quick, stateless operations such as running small mathematical calculations or looking up reference values, while other tasks require submitting long running simulation jobs that need high performance compute resources and containerized environments. The organization wants a unified framework that allows its foundation models to access both types of tools in a consistent way, while ensuring that lightweight tasks run with minimal latency and heavy simulation workloads run in scalable, dedicated compute environments. Which options can be combined to provide an efficient and extensible tool framework for both lightweight and heavy computational workloads? (Select two)

choices.text: Provision Amazon ECS based Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to host containerized simulation tools that require GPU acceleration or long running compute. Configure the foundation model to invoke these ECS based MCP servers through the same MCP interface used for lightweight tools to maintain consistency
choices.explanation: Provision Amazon ECS based Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to host containerized simulation tools that require GPU acceleration or long running compute. Configure the foundation model to invoke these ECS based MCP servers through the same MCP interface used for lightweight tools to maintain consistency ECS based MCP servers provide an ideal environment for resource intensive simulation workflows that require GPU acceleration, long processing times, custom libraries, or specialized containerized dependencies. By using Amazon ECS, you can run tools inside tailored container images that include the simulations, frameworks, and scientific libraries needed by researchers. ECS tasks can be configured to run on GPU instances, scale horizontally for distributed workloads, and execute for extended durations that would exceed Lambda service limits. Exposing these simulation tools as MCP compliant endpoints ensures that they appear identical to the foundation model, even though they run in a much heavier compute environment. This unified MCP interface eliminates the need for the model to manage separate invocation patterns and encourages the development of additional tools without modifying the application logic. The foundation model can issue complex requests through the same protocol while ECS handles compute intensive processing behind the scenes. This approach delivers a scalable, extensible, and operationally robust simulation framework aligned with the demands of scientific research environments.

choices.text: Deploy Lambda based Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to host stateless tool functions such as lightweight calculations and quick data lookups. Expose these Lambda based tools through MCP compliant interfaces
choices.explanation: Deploy Lambda based Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to host stateless tool functions such as lightweight calculations and quick data lookups. Expose these Lambda based tools through MCP compliant interfaces Using Lambda based MCP servers for lightweight tools provides the lowest latency and operationally simplest environment for fast calculations, lookup operations, and other stateless functions. Lambda is designed for rapid, event driven execution without the need for container orchestration or compute provisioning. This makes it ideal for tools that must respond instantly to model requests and perform tasks that finish within short execution windows. Exposing these Lambda functions as MCP compliant servers creates a standardized interface the foundation model can use without needing to know how or where each tool is hosted. This design enables a clean separation of tool responsibilities since Lambda automatically scales with request volume, handles concurrency for small workloads, and keeps cost proportional to actual usage. By integrating Lambda based MCP servers into the model extension framework, the system supports efficient invocations, minimal overhead, and rapid response times for all lightweight tasks. This ensures the GenAI workspace remains responsive and cost effective for functions that do not require heavy compute. AWS Serverless MCP Server: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/introducing-aws-serverless-mcp-server-ai-powered-development-for-modern-applications/

choices.text: Deploy Lambda functions to execute both lightweight tools and simulation workloads and increase memory and timeout limits for simulation tasks. Configure the foundation model to invoke Lambda directly for all operations without differentiating workload types
choices.explanation: Simulations cannot run efficiently on Lambda because of time limits, lack of GPU support, and container constraints. Increasing memory or timeout values does not resolve the need for specialized hardware and long running workloads.

choices.text: Deploy Lambda based Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for lightweight tools and use API Gateway endpoints to trigger simulations running on Amazon ECS. Configure the foundation model to use MCP only for quick utilities while calling API Gateway separately for simulation workloads
choices.explanation: Splitting interfaces by using MCP for lightweight tools and API Gateway for simulations creates an inconsistent access model for the foundation model. This breaks the unified tool invocation requirement and forces the model to treat simulation tasks differently, increasing complexity.

choices.text: Use a single Amazon ECS cluster to run Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for both quick calculations and GPU intensive simulations. Configure all requests to be processed through the same set of containerized tools regardless of workload size
choices.explanation: Running all MCP servers on a single ECS cluster forces lightweight tasks to incur container startup times, higher latency, and unnecessary cost. It also loses the elasticity and rapid response that Lambda provides for stateless operations and wastes GPU or CPU capacity on tasks that do not need it.

questions.question: A compliance team is building a retrieval augmented assistant using Amazon Bedrock to analyze thousands of long regulatory PDFs stored in Amazon S3. The PDFs contain complex structures such as headings, subheadings, tables, and footnotes, and early tests show that the assistant often retrieves partial clauses that miss important definitions and nearby contextual text. You need to redesign the document preprocessing flow so that chunking preserves the hierarchical structure of the documents while producing segments that work efficiently with Amazon Titan Text Embeddings and downstream vector search. What is the most effective way to redesign the document processing and chunking strategy?

choices.text: Use a Lambda function to split each PDF into fixed size text chunks and then let Amazon Titan Text Embeddings handle semantic interpretation. Then rely on the foundation model to reconstruct missing definitions and logical structure during inference
choices.explanation: Fixed size chunking ignores headings, definitions, and logical boundaries, which often causes embeddings to lose context or mix unrelated concepts. Relying on the foundation model to reconstruct missing structure leads to inconsistent retrieval for compliance focused content.

choices.text: Convert PDFs to structured formats such as HTML and upload them directly to Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. Leverage default ingestion pipeline to automatically infer hierarchical boundaries and generate Titan embeddings from the processed text
choices.explanation: This option seems correct because it uses structured preprocessing and Bedrock Knowledge Bases. However, it is incorrect because default KB ingestion does not guarantee preservation of hierarchical boundaries, especially in complex PDFs with nested definitions, long clauses, or multi level regulatory structures. Failing to configure custom hierarchical chunking rules results in inconsistent segmentation and lower retrieval precision.

choices.text: Convert PDFs into structured formats such as HTML and extract headings, subheadings, and table boundaries before ingestion. Then configure Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with hierarchical chunking rules and metadata mapping so that it creates structurally aligned segments for Titan embeddings and retrieval
choices.explanation: Convert PDFs into structured formats such as HTML and extract headings, subheadings, and table boundaries before ingestion. Then configure Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with hierarchical chunking rules and metadata mapping so that it creates structurally aligned segments for Titan embeddings and retrieval Using structured preprocessing combined with hierarchical chunking inside Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases creates well aligned segments that reflect the true structure of regulatory documents. Converting PDFs into HTML or similar structured formats allows the system to extract document landmarks such as headings, subheadings, paragraph blocks, and footnotes, enabling precise segmentation before ingestion. Configuring Bedrock Knowledge Bases with hierarchical boundaries ensures that its ingestion engine produces coherent chunks that preserve the relationships between definitions and their surrounding context. Hierarchical chunking organizes your data into a hierarchical structure, allowing for more granular and efficient retrieval based on the inherent relationships within your data: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/amazon-bedrock-knowledge-bases-now-supports-advanced-parsing-chunking-and-query-reformulation-giving-greater-control-of-accuracy-in-rag-based-applications/ Once these segments are passed through Amazon Titan Text Embeddings, the Knowledge Base stores both embeddings and metadata in a manner optimized for semantic retrieval. This setup significantly improves retrieval quality because the assistant can reference complete, context rich sections instead of partial or disconnected clauses.

choices.text: Upload entire documents directly into Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases without any preprocessing and leverage the default ingestion pipeline to identify chunk boundaries. Set up the Knowledge Base to automatically recover the logical relationships within the documents
choices.explanation: Default Knowledge Base chunking works for simple documents but does not reliably detect complex hierarchical boundaries within regulatory PDFs. Without preprocessing, the ingestion pipeline may produce inconsistent or incomplete segments that reduce retrieval precision.

questions.question: A development team is responsible for several microservices that handle prompt processing, request validation, and response formatting for their generative AI platform. Over time, different contributors have introduced varying coding styles, incomplete tests, inconsistent error handling, and inefficient client library patterns, creating maintenance challenges and repeated production defects. The team wants a consistent way to generate standard code structures, enforce internal architectural rules, detect SDK misusage, and produce reliable automated tests across all services without slowing development velocity. What do you recommend?

choices.text: Configure Amazon Q Developer to generate standardized boilerplate code, produce unit and integration tests, detect inefficient SDK usage, and automate code reviews based on internal architectural rules. Integrate Q Developer into the development workflow so that every pull request is evaluated consistently before approval
choices.explanation: Configure Amazon Q Developer to generate standardized boilerplate code, produce unit and integration tests, detect inefficient SDK usage, and automate code reviews based on internal architectural rules. Integrate Q Developer into the development workflow so that every pull request is evaluated consistently before approval A structured development workflow for generative AI services requires automated code generation, test creation, and review enforcement so that all microservices follow consistent patterns. Amazon Q Developer provides context-aware generation of boilerplate code, identifies inefficient API or SDK usage, and produces tests aligned with service behavior, allowing teams to accelerate delivery without sacrificing quality. By integrating Q Developer into pull requests, the team ensures that internal architecture rules are automatically validated and that code reviews remain consistent across contributors. This reduces defects, improves maintainability, and helps unify the development process for all microservices.

choices.text: Enable Amazon Q Developer to auto generate boilerplate code and basic test cases, and configure it to perform advisory reviews that flag potential deviations from internal guidelines. Allow developers to finalize code quality decisions manually so they can apply their own judgment during pull requests
choices.explanation: Relying on Amazon Q Developer only for advisory reviews does not enforce consistency across microservices because developers can bypass recommendations, leading to uneven code quality. Without mandatory automated reviews or strict enforcement of architectural rules, SDK usage issues and test coverage gaps continue to slip into production.

choices.text: Use Amazon Q Developer to generate initial code templates and run occasional static analysis reports, while relying on developers to perform manual code reviews and enforce architectural rules. Schedule Q Developer checks only during release cycles to validate overall code quality
choices.explanation: Occasional template generation and static analysis do not provide the continuous, automated enforcement needed for consistent microservice development. Without integrating Amazon Q Developer into the active review workflow, architectural rules, SDK optimization patterns, and test completeness cannot be reliably maintained.

choices.text: Integrate Amazon Q Developer only for generating service specific snippets and rely on github approval workflow to enforce architectural consistency across repositories. Use manual peer reviews to validate SDK usage patterns and ensure that all microservices follow the required design principles
choices.explanation: Using Amazon Q Developer solely for code snippets and depending on repository approval rules does not provide automated detection of inefficient SDK usage or comprehensive code quality checks. Without continuous AI assisted reviews or automated test generation, development inconsistencies persist across microservices.

questions.question: A wealth management firm is building a GenAI advisory engine that supports multiple personas such as relationship managers, compliance analysts, and portfolio strategists. Each persona requires a different combination of reasoning depth, context size, safety controls, and output format. The engine must meet strict real time latency budgets because clients often interact with advisors live, and the system must automatically route requests to the optimal foundation model based on persona and workload characteristics. The firm also needs a configuration approach that controls cost, applies consistent governance, and upholds compliance requirements for regulated financial environments. What are the most appropriate options for these requirements? (Select two)

choices.text: Deploy a set of persona optimized fine tuned models on Amazon SageMaker and use an API Gateway based router to select the endpoint for each persona. Fine tune each model for its domain specific needs
choices.explanation: Fine tuning multiple models on SageMaker requires maintaining multiple endpoints, scaling configurations, GPU resources, and container environments. This greatly increases operational overhead and cost, which contradicts the firm's need for efficiency and simplicity. It also makes governance more fragmented because each endpoint has its own configuration that must be tracked and audited.

choices.text: Use a single Claude 3 Sonnet model for all personas and dynamically adjust temperature, top p, and max tokens at runtime to allow flexibility in tuning responses without requiring multiple model configurations. Embed the persona logic directly into system prompts
choices.explanation: Using a single model and tuning inference parameters such as temperature and top p does not provide the necessary differentiation in reasoning depth, latency behavior, or cost per request. A single model will always have the same performance and computational requirements, which makes it unsuitable for latency critical personas. Embedding persona logic in prompts also reduces governance and creates maintenance challenges.

choices.text: Use Step Functions to orchestrate a branching workflow where each persona type triggers a specific path that calls the same foundation model with different prompt templates. Update the workflow definition when new personas are added or prompt templates are revised
choices.explanation: Step Functions are useful for workflow orchestration but do not optimize for low latency model selection or persona based routing. State machine transitions introduce latency and cannot dynamically evaluate model performance characteristics at runtime. This leads to a rigid architecture that does not match the firm's requirement for automatic, adaptive model routing based on workload constraints.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router with multiple persona specific model configurations so that each request is automatically directed to the optimal model
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router with multiple persona specific model configurations so that each request is automatically directed to the optimal model Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router is designed to intelligently route requests to the most appropriate foundation model configuration based on persona or input metadata. This allows the wealth management firm to create separate configurations for high reasoning workloads, such as those handled by portfolio strategists, and low latency workloads, such as those handled by compliance analysts. Because the routing logic is centrally managed, the firm avoids embedding persona selection logic inside the application layer and maintains a consistent governance and compliance posture across all personas. Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router is designed to intelligently route requests to the most appropriate foundation model configuration based on persona or input metadata. This allows the wealth management firm to create separate configurations for high reasoning workloads, such as those handled by portfolio strategists, and low latency workloads, such as those handled by compliance analysts. Because the routing logic is centrally managed, the firm avoids embedding persona selection logic inside the application layer and maintains a consistent governance and compliance posture across all personas. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/use-amazon-bedrock-intelligent-prompt-routing-for-cost-and-latency-benefits/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/use-amazon-bedrock-intelligent-prompt-routing-for-cost-and-latency-benefits/

choices.text: Leverage Bedrock Prompt Caching for frequently used advisory prompts and persona configurations
choices.explanation: Leverage Bedrock Prompt Caching for frequently used advisory prompts and persona configurations Prompt Caching helps handle high volume, frequently repeated advisory use cases in a cost efficient and low latency manner. Relationship managers and compliance analysts often ask similar patterns of questions such as standard portfolio overviews or policy checks, and these can benefit significantly from prompt caching. Prompt Caching stores the results of expensive model computations for repeated prompts, so subsequent invocations with the same prompt and relevant parameters can be served at lower cost and lower latency. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prompt-caching.html

questions.question: A bank is developing a GenAI assistant that handles customer queries about cards, loans, and savings products in a single chat interface. They want to detect the user’s intent as early as possible so that card disputes, loan preapproval checks, and account balance inquiries are routed to separate downstream workflows that each call different Amazon Bedrock models and prompts. The team decides to move away from simple keyword based routing and adopt ML based intent classification. As the lead GenAI engineer, how should you integrate Amazon services to detect intents and route each conversation to the correct handling logic?

choices.text: Store a keyword to intent mapping in Amazon DynamoDB and use a Lambda function to scan each user message for matching keywords. Route the request to the corresponding Amazon Bedrock workflow based on the first keyword match
choices.explanation: This option keeps the logic at a simple keyword matching layer and therefore does not implement ML based intent classification as requested. Keyword scanning in Lambda using a DynamoDB mapping can be brittle, misses nuanced phrasing, and does not scale well for complex banking intents.

choices.text: Train a custom classification model in Amazon Comprehend for card, loan, and savings intents and invoke it through an AWS Lambda function for each new user message. Use the Comprehend classification result to route the request to separate workflows that call the appropriate Amazon Bedrock model and prompt for each product domain
choices.explanation: Train a custom classification model in Amazon Comprehend for card, loan, and savings intents and invoke it through an AWS Lambda function for each new user message. Use the Comprehend classification result to route the request to separate workflows that call the appropriate Amazon Bedrock model and prompt for each product domain This approach uses Amazon Comprehend custom classification to detect intents such as card, loan, and savings in a data driven way, which directly addresses the need to move beyond keyword based routing. Invoking Comprehend through Lambda at the start of each interaction allows the system to classify user messages in real time and produce a clear intent label for routing. The application can then route each request to the appropriate downstream workflow that selects the correct Amazon Bedrock model and prompt for that domain. This design keeps intent detection, orchestration, and model selection cleanly separated while using managed ML services and scalable serverless components. Build a classification pipeline with Amazon Comprehend custom classification: via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html

choices.text: Configure Amazon Lex with separate bots for cards, loans, and savings and require users to select the correct bot at the start of the conversation. Have each Lex bot call the same Amazon Bedrock model but pass a different system prompt for its product area
choices.explanation: Amazon Lex can improve conversational interfaces but this option relies on users manually selecting the correct bot, which does not satisfy the requirement for early automatic intent detection. It also routes to the same Bedrock model with only prompt changes, which does not fully leverage separate workflows and models for different product domains.

choices.text: Train a custom classification model in Amazon Comprehend and configure a Lambda function to write each classification result into a shared DynamoDB table. Have separate card, loan, and savings microservices poll the table and invoke their own Amazon Bedrock models whenever they detect an intent that matches their domain
choices.explanation: This option introduces an inefficient architecture by writing intent results to DynamoDB and having multiple services poll the table. This design increases latency, duplicates routing logic, and complicates coordination, instead of letting a single routing layer synchronously direct traffic to the correct Bedrock workflow.

questions.question: A media company is using GenAI to generate article summaries and title suggestions. Product managers want to experiment with multiple foundation models and different prompt templates in parallel, then route traffic based on automatic quality scores and user engagement metrics. They require an orchestrated workflow that can run model A versus model B, log metrics, update experiment configuration, and gradually roll out the best performing variant. How should a GenAI developer build a solution to support automated evaluation and controlled rollout of multiple model variants?

choices.text: Implements an AWS Step Functions workflow that invokes multiple GenAI model variants in parallel and writes basic results to CloudWatch Logs while using static routing weights configured in environment variables. Run a separate step to read the logs and notify the product managers when a variant appears to perform better, and the product managers then manually update the routing configuration
choices.explanation: This option is incorrect because it stops halfway to a true automated experiment and rollout system. The design uses Step Functions with static routing weights and relies on product managers to read CloudWatch Logs and manually change configuration, so traffic is not actually shifted by the workflow based on metrics. The best solution would use Standard workflows where needed, write structured metrics to a dedicated store (for example CloudWatch metrics or a custom table), and have the state machine or a companion process update routing weights in a controlled, programmatic way instead of treating logs as the decision source.

choices.text: Implement an AWS Step Functions workflow that invokes multiple GenAI model variants in parallel, writes quality scores and engagement signals to a metrics store, and updates routing weights based on evaluation results. Use CloudWatch metrics and alarms to track variant performance and gradually shift traffic to the best performing configuration
choices.explanation: Implement an AWS Step Functions workflow that invokes multiple GenAI model variants in parallel, writes quality scores and engagement signals to a metrics store, and updates routing weights based on evaluation results. Use CloudWatch metrics and alarms to track variant performance and gradually shift traffic to the best performing configuration This solution uses AWS Step Functions to orchestrate calls to multiple GenAI model variants in parallel branches and to centralize the logic for scoring and routing. Each execution can send the same input to model A and model B, compute or retrieve quality scores, write engagement and outcome metrics to a store such as CloudWatch metrics, DynamoDB, or a feature store, and then update routing weights or configuration parameters that downstream services use to decide which model to call for live traffic. Step Functions provides a clear view of each experiment run and allows the architect to add states for reading and updating experiment configuration so that rollouts can advance gradually. CloudWatch metrics and alarms can track variant performance over time, and the workflow can include steps that adjust traffic weights or write new configuration to a shared store when a variant consistently outperforms others. This pattern supports automated A versus B testing loops and controlled rollout by encoding the experiment lifecycle as a state machine rather than relying on manual updates.

choices.text: Build an Amazon SageMaker Pipeline that only trains and registers a single model at a time and deploys it directly to production without traffic splitting. Product managers run separate offline experiments in notebooks and manually choose which model or prompt to use in the next pipeline run
choices.explanation: A SageMaker Pipeline that always trains, registers, and deploys a single model at a time focuses on model lifecycle, not online experimentation across multiple active variants. Product managers would still need to run separate offline experiments and manually choose winners, which does not meet the requirement for an orchestrated workflow that runs variants in parallel and routes traffic based on live metrics.

choices.text: Creates an Amazon SQS queue that feeds a fleet of EC2 instances, where each instance randomly selects a model and prompt for each request and writes results to CloudWatch Logs. Product managers periodically download logs and calculate variant performance in spreadsheets before deciding which configuration to keep
choices.explanation: Randomizing model and prompt selection inside EC2 workers driven by SQS creates a basic experiment environment but lacks centralized orchestration, clear experiment state, or automated rollout control. Performance analysis would depend on manual log exports and spreadsheets, which slows decision making and makes it harder to add rules that gradually shift traffic to the best variant.

questions.question: A financial services company uses a mix of third-party and custom models for different GenAI workloads, such as summarization, document classification, and code generation. Some workloads can use managed foundation models through Amazon Bedrock, while others must run on custom fine-tuned models hosted on SageMaker AI for compliance reasons. The company wants a single API layer that can route each request to the most appropriate model based on task type, latency requirements, and cost profiles. How should you design a hybrid deployment strategy and orchestration layer that integrates Amazon Bedrock and SageMaker AI endpoints in a secure and observable way?

choices.text: Create a routing layer that inspects each request and directs it to either Amazon Bedrock or SageMaker AI endpoints based on the task type, latency needs, and cost constraints. Integrate IAM, VPC connectivity, and CloudWatch logging so that all model calls are secured and monitored across both backends
choices.explanation: Create a routing layer that inspects each request and directs it to either Amazon Bedrock or SageMaker AI endpoints based on the task type, latency needs, and cost constraints. Integrate IAM, VPC connectivity, and CloudWatch logging so that all model calls are secured and monitored across both backends The company can implement a small routing layer using Amazon API Gateway and a Lambda function or an ECS microservice that acts as the single entry point for all generative AI requests. When an application calls this API, the routing layer inspects the request details and decides whether to invoke a managed model on Amazon Bedrock or a custom fine tuned model on a SageMaker AI endpoint. The routing service runs inside a VPC and connects privately to both Bedrock and SageMaker using VPC endpoints, while IAM roles restrict which models can be invoked. All routing decisions, performance metrics, and errors are recorded in CloudWatch so the team can monitor latency, trace model usage, and meet compliance requirements. This setup provides a unified, secure, and intelligent interface that automatically selects the most appropriate model for each workload.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock as the primary interface and call SageMaker AI endpoints only when Bedrock models fail or return low quality output. Depend on application retries to manage routing decisions and rely on default monitoring for tracking all model interactions
choices.explanation: Using Bedrock as the default and routing to SageMaker AI only when calls fail reduces control over model selection and may degrade performance and compliance guarantees. Relying on retries also increases latency and cost while failing to provide structured routing logic aligned to business needs.

choices.text: Expose separate APIs for Bedrock and SageMaker AI models and instruct application teams to choose the correct backend for each workload. Use standard VPC networking without unified monitoring to keep the architecture simple
choices.explanation: Exposing separate APIs for each model backend forces application teams to understand and manage different hosting platforms. Without unified routing and centralized monitoring, the architecture becomes fragmented and cannot meet compliance or observability expectations.

choices.text: Host all third party and internal models on SageMaker AI multi model endpoints to simplify integration and remove the need for orchestration. Let applications specify model names directly so they can decide which model to invoke during runtime
choices.explanation: Large external models hosted on Amazon Bedrock cannot be moved into SageMaker AI multi model endpoints, and multi model endpoints are intended for smaller models loaded dynamically. Allowing applications to choose models directly breaks governance and does not support centralized routing or compliance requirements.

questions.question: A healthcare analytics group is preparing structured clinical data and semi structured notes to use as a retrieval source for an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base. Regulatory officers insist that certain diagnosis codes and patient identifiers must only be visible to a small compliance group, while de-identified aggregates can be used by the general data science team. What do you recommend?

choices.text: Store the clinical datasets in S3 with default bucket level permissions and use IAM identity policies to restrict access to sensitive diagnosis codes. Allow the Bedrock Knowledge Base to retrieve data directly from S3 objects while relying on IAM to enforce data visibility rules at runtime
choices.explanation: Relying on IAM alone cannot enforce fine grained controls such as column level or row level permissions for regulated healthcare datasets. S3 bucket permissions are too coarse and do not support the required granular filtering needed before ingestion into a Bedrock Knowledge Base.

choices.text: Enable Lake Formation for the clinical data catalog, apply table level permissions and leverage Amazon Athena views to mask sensitive fields. Grant both the compliance and data science teams access to the same tables and use SQL filters to remove restricted records during retrieval operations
choices.explanation: Using only table level permissions with Athena views leaves gaps because users could still access restricted columns or rows through underlying tables. SQL level masking is not a substitute for Lake Formation column, row, or cell level controls and cannot guarantee compliance for sensitive clinical data.

choices.text: Configure Lake Formation with column level, row level, and cell level permissions on the clinical datasets and grant fine grained access only to the compliance group for sensitive diagnosis codes and patient identifiers. Allow the data science team to access de-identified columns and aggregated rows while enabling the Bedrock Knowledge Base to query only the permitted views
choices.explanation: Configure Lake Formation with column level, row level, and cell level permissions on the clinical datasets and grant fine grained access only to the compliance group for sensitive diagnosis codes and patient identifiers. Allow the data science team to access de-identified columns and aggregated rows while enabling the Bedrock Knowledge Base to query only the permitted views Lake Formation provides granular governance controls for structured and semi structured data that can restrict access at the table, column, row, and cell levels. Applying these permissions to diagnosis codes, identifiers, and other sensitive fields ensures that only the compliance group can access regulated data while maintaining full audit visibility. Creating separate access paths for aggregated or de-identified columns allows the general data science team to use the retrieval system without compromising patient privacy. The Bedrock Knowledge Base will only query the permitted views, ensuring that the GenAI assistant operates within the proper compliance boundaries. Highly recommend the following deep-dive on implementing cell-level and row-level security using AWS Lake Formation: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/part-1-effective-data-lakes-using-aws-lake-formation-implementing-cell-level-and-row-level-security/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/part-2-effective-data-lakes-using-aws-lake-formation-secure-data-lakes-with-row-level-access-control/

choices.text: Configure Lake Formation with table level access grants and create two Data Catalog entries that separate sensitive and non sensitive clinical fields. Allow the compliance group to query both catalogs while exposing only the non sensitive catalog to the data science team. Leverage Bedrock Knowledge Bases to automatically filter restricted cells during retrieval
choices.explanation: Creating separate Data Catalog entries does not provide true column level, row level, or cell level enforcement because users with table level permission can still access full datasets unless fine grained Lake Formation controls are applied.
