Bedrock Knowledge Bases cannot filter restricted cells on behalf of Lake Formation, so sensitive diagnosis codes and identifiers may still be exposed if the underlying datasets are not governed at the required granularity.

questions.question: A customer support organization receives thousands of unstructured issue descriptions each day, many of which contain sensitive personal details such as phone numbers, home addresses, and customer account identifiers. The team wants to build an automated summarization workflow that can generate concise and accurate summaries without exposing the raw sensitive data to the language model. The leadership mandates that sensitive elements must be identified, transformed, or removed before the summarization step, and that downstream teams should be able to audit how personal data was handled. Which of the following represents the best solution?

choices.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to detect PII entities and send both the redacted and original text to the foundation model so that it can choose the best version for summarization. Store the original content in a secondary location and rely on IAM policies to prevent unauthorized access
choices.explanation: Sending both sanitized and raw text to the model violates the requirement that no unmasked PII should enter the summarization step. Although Comprehend detects sensitive fields, the presence of the original content in the model input breaks the core privacy constraint.

choices.text: Integrate Amazon Comprehend PII detection as a preprocessing step and use a Lambda function to automatically redact or replace detected PII entities before passing the sanitized text to the foundation model. Configure the workflow so that only redacted versions are forwarded while logging detection metadata for compliance audits
choices.explanation: Integrate Amazon Comprehend PII detection as a preprocessing step and use a Lambda function to automatically redact or replace detected PII entities before passing the sanitized text to the foundation model. Configure the workflow so that only redacted versions are forwarded while logging detection metadata for compliance audits Amazon Comprehend PII detection can identify more than 25 categories of personal information and is designed for integration inside preprocessing pipelines. Using a Lambda function to transform or redact each detected entity ensures that only sanitized text is delivered to the model, meeting compliance requirements and operationalizing a repeatable privacy control. Logging redaction details and maintaining audit visibility allows compliance teams to verify how specific pieces of personal information were handled before summarization. This architecture ensures that sensitive data is never exposed to the foundation model while preserving enough context for support agents to use the summaries effectively. How to redact PII with Amazon Comprehend: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/detecting-and-redacting-pii-using-amazon-comprehend/

choices.text: Integrate Amazon Comprehend PII detection into the workflow and tag detected entities. Keep the original text intact so the foundation model can use full context during summarization. Store the redaction metadata separately and rely on downstream systems to mask sensitive fields after the model completes its output
choices.explanation: Tagging PII without redacting it allows the original sensitive data to flow directly into the foundation model, which violates the requirement that no raw personal information should reach the model. Even though Comprehend identifies sensitive content, delaying masking until after model processing fails to enforce privacy controls at the point of ingestion.

choices.text: Implement a data masking process using CloudWatch Logs Insights that scans stored support tickets and replaces sensitive values after the content has already been processed by the model. Forward the model generated summaries to downstream systems without additional filtering steps
choices.explanation: Replacing sensitive values only after the model has already processed them does not protect the model from ingesting PII. Post processing transformations cannot meet compliance demands because the sensitive content has already reached the model during the initial summarization step.

questions.question: A financial services company is building an internal GenAI assistant on Amazon Bedrock that will process highly sensitive transaction narratives and dispute descriptions. The security team requires that all Bedrock traffic stays entirely within their AWS network and that only specific application subnets can call the Bedrock APIs, with no exposure to the public internet. How should you design the network architecture to meet these isolation requirements while still allowing the assistant to scale across multiple Availability Zones?

choices.text: Use a NAT gateway for all outbound Bedrock API calls and restrict NAT access to only the application subnets. Enable cross Availability Zone failover by attaching additional route table entries that direct Bedrock traffic through the NAT infrastructure
choices.explanation: A VPC endpoint service is used when you are the service provider, not the consumer. You create a VPC endpoint service so that other VPCs can privately connect to your application using PrivateLink. A VPC endpoint service cannot front Amazon Bedrock because Bedrock is a managed AWS service that requires an interface VPC endpoint, not a custom VPC endpoint service. Routing through internal DNS rules would not provide the guaranteed private connectivity path required for regulated workloads and does not satisfy the requirement to keep traffic fully inside the AWS network.

choices.text: Create interface VPC endpoints for Amazon Bedrock and attach security groups that allow access from all subnets in the VPC for simplified management. Deploy the assistant across multiple Availability Zones and rely on default VPC routing to maintain private connectivity
choices.explanation: Although interface VPC endpoints are the correct mechanism for enabling private connectivity to Amazon Bedrock, using overly permissive security groups does not satisfy the requirement that only designated application subnets may call the Bedrock APIs. Allowing all subnets to access the endpoint violates the principle of least privilege and fails to meet the financial institution's strict isolation controls, even though traffic stays inside the VPC.

choices.text: Configure interface VPC endpoints for Amazon Bedrock using AWS PrivateLink and attach tightly scoped security groups that allow only approved application subnets to connect. Deploy the GenAI assistant across multiple Availability Zones to ensure scalable and private connectivity without any public internet access
choices.explanation: Configure interface VPC endpoints for Amazon Bedrock using AWS PrivateLink and attach tightly scoped security groups that allow only approved application subnets to connect. Deploy the GenAI assistant across multiple Availability Zones to ensure scalable and private connectivity without any public internet access An interface VPC endpoint is an entry point inside your VPC that allows your VPC resources to privately connect to AWS managed services such as Amazon Bedrock. Using interface VPC endpoints for Amazon Bedrock ensures that all traffic to the service stays within the AWS network and never traverses the public internet. Security groups attached to the endpoint can allow only specific application subnets to initiate Bedrock API calls, meeting strict financial services isolation requirements. Deploying the assistant across multiple Availability Zones ensures high availability and elasticity while still keeping all traffic private. AWS PrivateLink automatically scales with the VPC endpoint, providing consistent connectivity without exposing any component to public networks. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/use-aws-privatelink-to-set-up-private-access-to-amazon-bedrock/ via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/use-aws-privatelink-to-set-up-private-access-to-amazon-bedrock/

choices.text: Create a VPC endpoint service for the application and attach security groups that restrict which subnets can connect while routing Bedrock traffic through the service. Configure the assistant to access Bedrock using internal DNS rules so that traffic does not require direct public connectivity
choices.explanation: Using a NAT gateway still exposes traffic paths that depend on public network infrastructure and does not meet the requirement of fully private connectivity to Amazon Bedrock. NAT cannot restrict access to specific models or endpoints at the network layer, and it introduces unnecessary cost and operational overhead for cross AZ scaling.

questions.question: A company has deployed a generative AI application that processes thousands of user requests per hour. Recently, users have begun reporting inconsistent behavior that is difficult to reproduce, including occasional fabricated responses and unpredictable spikes in response time. The engineering team believes that these issues may be linked to hidden changes in prompt characteristics, upstream data quality, and execution variability that occur only under specific traffic patterns. Which solution should the team implement for end to end observability?

choices.text: Configure CloudWatch metrics to track average and maximum latency, store prompts in a static S3 bucket without versioning, and rely on periodic X Ray sampling at default settings. Use CloudWatch alarms that notify the team when request duration exceeds a defined threshold
choices.explanation: Tracking latency and storing prompts without metadata or versioning provides only superficial insight. Without token level annotations, prompt lineage, or synthetic testing, the team cannot correlate hallucinations or spikes to specific prompt patterns or model behaviors.

choices.text: Use OpenSearch dashboards to visualize response trends, configure basic application logs without prompt metadata, and manage prompt changes through ad hoc developer notes. Restart the application services when latency or hallucination patterns are observed
choices.explanation: General dashboards and unstructured logging lack the depth needed for prompt level and token level diagnostics. Without metadata rich observability or automated test workloads, the root causes of hallucinations and latency instability remain hidden.

choices.text: Run a nightly batch job that analyzes logged prompts for hallucination patterns, configure an EventBridge rule to alert when error counts increase, and use CloudWatch to track high level model invocation metrics
choices.explanation: Nightly batch analysis and high level metrics do not capture the nuanced, intermittent failures characteristic of generative model hallucinations. Without real time tracing of prompt attributes or continuous synthetic checks, the team cannot detect or prevent emerging issues.

choices.text: Build an end-to-end pipeline that centralizes all prompts, responses, and versions in a prompt registry, applies CloudWatch Logs Insights queries to detect token usage and response quality anomalies, and enables X Ray tracing with custom annotations to correlate latency with prompt metadata. Establish continuous synthetic monitoring that executes representative test prompts to detect performance degradation
choices.explanation: Build an end-to-end pipeline that centralizes all prompts, responses, and versions in a prompt registry, applies CloudWatch Logs Insights queries to detect token usage and response quality anomalies, and enables X Ray tracing with custom annotations to correlate latency with prompt metadata. Establish continuous synthetic monitoring that executes representative test prompts to detect performance degradation A complete observability framework for generative AI must correlate prompts, token usage, latency, and output quality to diagnose issues like hallucinations and performance spikes. A centralized prompt registry creates versioned traceability, while CloudWatch Logs Insights enables analysis of structured logs that contain token counts, truncated prompts, and generation metadata. X Ray tracing with custom annotations adds visibility into how prompt attributes and model invocation behavior influence end-to-end latency. Synthetic monitoring provides continuous validation by sending representative prompts that surface degradation early and reveal intermittent issues that occur only under specific load or prompt complexity patterns. Together, these components deliver a unified view of model behavior, allowing the engineering team to isolate root causes quickly and prevent future instability. What is AWS X-Ray?: via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/aws-xray.html

questions.question: A SaaS provider exposes a text analysis API to hundreds of external customers that internally calls Amazon Bedrock for classification and sentiment analysis. Some customers send prompts that exceed model token limits or omit required fields, while others generate sudden traffic spikes that overload the downstream model endpoint. The provider wants to enforce strict schema validation for request bodies and parameters and apply tiered throttling per customer plan before any request reaches Bedrock. Which options do you recommend? (Select two)

choices.text: Configure Amazon API Gateway with request validators and models that use JSON Schema to enforce required fields and token limit constraints on the text analysis API before invoking an AWS Lambda function that calls Amazon Bedrock
choices.explanation: Configure Amazon API Gateway with request validators and models that use JSON Schema to enforce required fields and token limit constraints on the text analysis API before invoking an AWS Lambda function that calls Amazon Bedrock Using API Gateway request validators and models with JSON Schema allows the SaaS provider to reject malformed payloads and enforce limits on fields such as token counts before any integration code runs. This ensures that only well formed requests with the required fields and acceptable sizes are passed to the Lambda function that invokes Amazon Bedrock, which protects the downstream model and simplifies error handling.

choices.text: Implement throttling with Lambda reserved concurrency so that burst traffic is absorbed by the function
choices.explanation: Relying on Lambda concurrency settings instead of API Gateway usage plans does not provide per customer throttling or tiered rate limits, so the provider cannot easily enforce different limits for each subscription plan.

choices.text: Place an Application Load Balancer in front of the text analysis service and attach AWS WAF with rate based rules to block high request rates from abusive clients. Rely on the downstream Lambda function that calls Amazon Bedrock to handle JSON parsing, required field checks, and token limit enforcement for all customers
choices.explanation: An Application Load Balancer with AWS WAF rate based rules can mitigate abusive traffic patterns, but it does not provide built in JSON Schema based validation of request bodies or per tenant usage plans. Leaving all field validation and token limit checks to the downstream Lambda function means malformed or oversized requests can still pass through the edge and waste backend capacity.

choices.text: Use API keys and usage plans in API Gateway to define per customer throttling limits for each subscription tier so that traffic is controlled before it reaches the downstream model endpoint
choices.explanation: Use API keys and usage plans in API Gateway to define per customer throttling limits for each subscription tier so that traffic is controlled before it reaches the downstream model endpoint API keys and usage plans in API Gateway are designed to implement tenant aware throttling and quotas, so the provider can define different request rates and burst limits for each customer plan and enforce them at the edge. This combination gives fine grained control over both schema validation and traffic shaping directly at the API layer, which aligns exactly with the requirement to protect Bedrock from invalid input and sudden spikes. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-api-usage-plans.html

choices.text: Use Amazon API Gateway with a Lambda integration that performs all request validation inside the function code and relies on CloudWatch alarms to detect customers who exceed token limits or send malformed payloads
choices.explanation: Pushing all validation into the Lambda function means invalid requests still consume compute resources and can reach the integration layer, which is less efficient than rejecting them at the API Gateway edge with request validation. CloudWatch alarms only detect issues after they occur and cannot block malformed or oversized requests before they reach Lambda or Bedrock. They do not provide per customer throttling or enforce JSON schema validation, so they cannot satisfy the requirement for pre invocation protection at the API boundary.

questions.question: A global financial organization is developing an internal GenAI assistant to help compliance and risk analysts query large volumes of regulatory filings, internal policies, procedural documentation, and structured summaries stored across multiple enterprise content systems. The documents are updated frequently to reflect regulatory changes across regions, and analysts often submit long, multi-part questions that combine jurisdiction, regulation version, exception handling, and historical interpretation. The solution must scale to large document collections and support configurable document chunking. During testing, the organization observes that while responses are factually correct, some answers reference passages that are only partially aligned with the full intent of complex analyst queries. The engineering team wants to improve how relevant context is selected for answer generation without modifying foundation models, building custom ML pipelines, or increasing operational complexity. The solution must rely on managed services and integrate cleanly into the existing retrieval workflow. Which solution should the team implement to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Configure the Knowledge Base to retrieve a broad set of candidate chunks using an embedding model, then enable the Bedrock managed reranking capability to reorder retrieved chunks before they are passed to the foundation model. Tune chunk size and overlap to balance recall and context density while relying on the reranker to prioritize the most semantically relevant passages
choices.explanation: Configure the Knowledge Base to retrieve a broad set of candidate chunks using an embedding model, then enable the Bedrock managed reranking capability to reorder retrieved chunks before they are passed to the foundation model. Tune chunk size and overlap to balance recall and context density while relying on the reranker to prioritize the most semantically relevant passages This approach aligns closely with how Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases are designed to improve Retrieval Augmented Generation quality at production scale. The architecture intentionally separates fast, high-recall retrieval from precision relevance scoring so that large document collections can be queried efficiently without sacrificing answer quality. By first retrieving a broad candidate set, the system ensures that potentially relevant context is not prematurely excluded when queries contain multiple constraints or nuanced conditions. Vector search is responsible for preserving recall by quickly identifying a sufficiently large pool of candidate chunks based on embedding similarity. After this retrieval step, the managed reranking capability evaluates the semantic relevance of each candidate in the context of the full user query and reorders the results accordingly. This reranking step occurs strictly before generation, which improves the quality of grounded context provided to the foundation model without requiring any changes to the model itself or introducing custom machine learning pipelines. Additionally, this design maintains full operational simplicity. Document chunking strategies such as chunk size and overlap remain configurable, allowing teams to tune context granularity based on document structure and query patterns. Because both retrieval and reranking are provided as managed capabilities, the pipeline remains scalable, maintainable, and consistent with AWS best practices for GenAI workloads, while avoiding additional infrastructure or operational burden. Highly recommend this deep-dive on improving AI assistant response accuracy using Knowledge Bases for Amazon Bedrock and a reranking model: via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/improve-ai-assistant-response-accuracy-using-knowledge-bases-for-amazon-bedrock-and-a-reranking-model/

choices.text: Reduce chunk size aggressively and increase the vector similarity threshold so that only highly similar embeddings are returned from the vector store. This minimizes noisy context and removes the need for reranking while keeping the retrieval pipeline simple
choices.explanation: This option sounds operationally efficient but confuses filtering with ranking. Tight similarity thresholds reduce recall and can exclude relevant context entirely, especially for long, multi-constraint queries. It fails the requirement to preserve high recall and does not address semantic ordering issues.

choices.text: Generate multiple candidate responses from the foundation model using different temperature and top-p values, then apply a reranker model to score the generated answers and return the best response to the analyst
choices.explanation: This is a classic GenAI distractor. Bedrock reranking applies to retrieved documents, not to model-generated outputs. Reranking responses requires custom orchestration and is not supported natively by Knowledge Bases, violating the requirement to avoid custom pipelines.

choices.text: Replace vector similarity search with a cross-encoder reranker model that directly scores every document chunk against the user query at query time. This ensures maximum relevance by eliminating approximate nearest neighbor search

questions.question: A media company’s content generation system must remain available across multiple geographic regions. During high traffic periods, Amazon Bedrock occasionally returns “Too many requests” errors for their preferred model. The company wants to increase resilience without modifying the application’s content generation logic or switching to a different foundation model family. They want an approach that preserves model consistency while ensuring that surges in regional traffic do not cause user facing failures. Which model configuration strategy should they choose?

choices.text: Increase the maximum number of provisioned inference units for the model in a single Region and rely on autoscaling to handle peak load coming from other Regions
choices.explanation: Increasing provisioned inference units in a single Region may help with predictable workloads but does not guarantee relief during sudden traffic spikes or regional capacity shortages. Provisioned capacity also increases cost and does not provide any cross Region failover capability. If the Region experiences service side throttling, the system will still receive “Too many requests” errors.

choices.text: Configure a Step Functions workflow that retries failed model invocations with exponential backoff and replays requests until the throttling condition clears
choices.explanation: Using Step Functions to manage retries and exponential backoff increases latency and does not provide a reliable solution when the throttling is caused by regional capacity constraints. The workflow can help with transient failures, but it cannot redirect traffic to another Region or guarantee availability during heavy load. This results in longer response times and potentially failed content generation sessions.

choices.text: Enable Amazon Bedrock cross Region inference so that requests automatically fail over to another Region when the primary Region experiences throttling
choices.explanation: Enable Amazon Bedrock cross Region inference so that requests automatically fail over to another Region when the primary Region experiences throttling Amazon Bedrock cross Region inference provides built in fault tolerance by allowing model requests to automatically route to a secondary Region when a primary Region experiences throttling or capacity constraints. This approach preserves the existing model configuration and the company’s preferred model family without requiring any changes in application logic. Because the failover occurs at the Bedrock level, the content generation engine continues to function normally even when the primary Region is overloaded. Cross Region inference aligns closely with the company’s goals of improving resilience while avoiding architectural disruptions. It does not require the team to build custom routing workflows, error handling logic, or multi Region orchestration. The failover behavior is centrally managed and transparent to the application, which helps maintain consistent output quality and availability during traffic spikes. This approach also reduces operational burden and ensures the company does not need to migrate to alternate model families during incidents.

choices.text: Use Amazon CloudFront to route inference traffic through an edge distribution layer before the requests reach Amazon Bedrock from all Regions
choices.explanation: CloudFront cannot cache or distribute model inference requests in a way that reduces Bedrock throttling. Model inference is dynamic and unique to each request, so CloudFront adds architectural complexity without providing real benefits for handling Bedrock capacity constraints.

questions.question: An insurance company’s AI governance committee meets monthly to review the compliance status of all foundation model workloads across multiple Regions and business units. They want automated reports that summarize key governance metrics, such as model card coverage, lineage completeness, bias and drift incidents, guardrail enforcement statistics, and remediation actions taken, without assembling data manually from many teams. As a GenAI developer, how would you design an architecture that aggregates monitoring and governance data into automated, consumable compliance reports?

choices.text: Export model monitoring and governance logs directly from each Region to Amazon S3 and require compliance analysts to run Athena queries on demand to assemble dashboards. Configure CloudWatch Alarms to notify analysts when new log files arrive so they know when to refresh the reports
choices.explanation: Exporting logs and requiring analysts to manually assemble reports introduces operational overhead and delays, which contradicts the requirement for automated, consumable compliance reports. CloudWatch Alarms only notify analysts but do not build structured dashboards or consolidate governance metrics across Regions.

choices.text: Aggregate governance metrics from SageMaker Model Monitor, Clarify, and lineage APIs into Amazon CloudWatch. To improve processing time, keep the raw CloudWatch logs directly in Amazon S3 without any ETL or data normalization. Use Amazon QuickSight to query the unprocessed logs and build compliance dashboards that reviewers refresh manually each month
choices.explanation: This option is incorrect because it provides no ETL, normalization, or structured metric preparation layer. QuickSight cannot reliably query raw CloudWatch logs for complex governance metrics, and manual monthly refresh operations do not meet the requirement for automated, consumable compliance reporting.

choices.text: Aggregate governance metrics from SageMaker Model Monitor, SageMaker Clarify, lineage APIs, and Bedrock Guardrails into Amazon CloudWatch and publish them to a central account through cross account metrics. Use scheduled AWS Glue ETL jobs to prepare these metrics in Amazon S3 and visualize automated monthly compliance reports in Amazon QuickSight
choices.explanation: Aggregate governance metrics from SageMaker Model Monitor, SageMaker Clarify, lineage APIs, and Bedrock Guardrails into Amazon CloudWatch and publish them to a central account through cross account metrics. Use scheduled AWS Glue ETL jobs to prepare these metrics in Amazon S3 and visualize automated monthly compliance reports in Amazon QuickSight This solution unifies governance metrics from monitoring, lineage, model documentation, and guardrail systems into CloudWatch, which can act as a central telemetry hub across multiple Regions and accounts. AWS Glue ETL jobs can standardize these metrics, enrich them with compliance context, and store them in Amazon S3 as a clean reporting layer. Automating dashboards in Amazon QuickSight allows the governance committee to receive consistent, consolidated reports without manual data collection. This architecture supports recurring reviews, enterprise wide visibility, and scalable compliance analytics for foundation model workloads.

choices.text: Store lineage metadata, model card documents, and bias metrics in separate DynamoDB tables per business unit and run a weekly Lambda function to push table snapshots into a reporting bucket. Provide auditors with direct S3 access so they can download and compile reports manually
choices.explanation: Storing governance data in siloed DynamoDB tables creates fragmentation and requires manual compilation, making it difficult to produce unified compliance reports. This design does not automate metric aggregation across Regions and does not support centralized dashboards. Reference: Amazon DynamoDB

questions.question: A large enterprise wants to build a centralized GenAI platform that supports HR, Legal, Sales, and Engineering teams with a single unified architecture. Each department requires different prompt templates, foundation model choices, safety configurations, and contextual data sources that are aligned to their specific workflows. The platform must enforce strong centralized governance so that changes to prompts, models, or data access rules are controlled through consistent policies. The organization also wants to ensure that safety guardrails are uniformly applied across all teams and that the architecture avoids custom container deployments or logic duplication across departments. Which architectural approach should the enterprise adopt to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Integrate all departmental workloads into a single Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base so that HR, Legal, Sales, and Engineering data exists in one shared vector index. Use a shared Claude model to retrieve context from the Knowledge Base and generate unified outputs for all teams. Implement prompt variations by tagging documents inside the Knowledge Base
choices.explanation: A single Knowledge Base for all departments does not provide the isolation or governance required for HR, Legal, Sales, and Engineering content. Knowledge Bases are not intended to act as a universal cross department storage system, and using a single model with a single KB would result in excessive data mixing and governance challenges. The tagging approach also does not replace the need for prompt level configuration and model specific routing.

choices.text: Use API Gateway to route traffic to a unified Lambda based microservice that contains personalized logic for each department. Store prompt templates and safety configurations inside DynamoDB and maintain separate routing rules for each business unit. Execute direct calls to the Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel API for inference
choices.explanation: A single Lambda microservice with custom routing logic centralizes code but does not centralize governance. It also forces developers to embed departmental prompt templates, safety rules, and routing decisions directly into microservice logic, which becomes difficult to maintain at enterprise scale. This approach shifts governance responsibility into Lambda code rather than using a managed control plane such as Bedrock Prompt Router or Flows.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router to route each department’s requests to the appropriate model configuration and combine this with Bedrock Guardrails to apply department specific safety rules. Orchestrate prompt assembly and retrieval using Amazon Bedrock Flows, allowing centralized governance and consistent policy enforcement
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router to route each department’s requests to the appropriate model configuration and combine this with Bedrock Guardrails to apply department specific safety rules. Orchestrate prompt assembly and retrieval using Amazon Bedrock Flows, allowing centralized governance and consistent policy enforcement Amazon Bedrock Prompt Router provides a fully managed routing layer that directs each incoming request to a specific foundation model configuration based on departmental rules. This allows the HR department to use a lightweight model for employee policy questions, while the Legal department can use a reasoning focused model for contract summarization. Using Prompt Router ensures that these routing rules remain centralized, governed, and versioned, which prevents departments from creating isolated routing logic or maintaining their own infrastructure. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prompt-routing.html Amazon Bedrock Flows can orchestrate additional steps such as assembling department specific prompt templates, enriching users with retrieval, and applying safety policies consistently. This creates a single control plane for model selection, prompt governance, and guardrail enforcement. The combination of Prompt Router, Guardrails, and Flows delivers a fully managed and centrally governed solution that avoids duplicated logic, container maintenance, and custom routing code. It is the most streamlined and maintainable architecture for a multi department GenAI platform. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html

choices.text: Deploy separate multi model endpoints in Amazon SageMaker, each hosting a different set of foundation models for HR, Legal, Sales, and Engineering. Maintain prompt templates and safety settings for each endpoint through custom code stored within the inference container. Invoke the appropriate endpoint based on metadata passed in each request via the application layer
choices.explanation: Using SageMaker multi model endpoints introduces unnecessary operational burden because each business unit would require its own containerized model hosting environment. This results in duplicated prompt logic, duplicated safety logic, and a fragmented governance model. The enterprise would also have to maintain container deployments, scaling policies, and prompt versioning processes manually. AWS documentation for SageMaker multi model endpoints:

questions.question: A retail analytics company operates a recommendation API that relies on Amazon OpenSearch Service to provide real-time product suggestions. The system stores more than 8 million vector embeddings and processes thousands of concurrent queries during peak shopping periods. Recently, the development team has observed significant query latency and inconsistent response times due to index size, suboptimal shard configurations, and insufficient optimization of approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search parameters. Which optimization strategy should the team adopt? (Select two)

choices.text: Migrate all vector embeddings to Bedrock Knowledge Bases and use managed retrieval to replace the OpenSearch index
choices.explanation: Although Bedrock Knowledge Bases provides a managed vector store, using it to replace OpenSearch does not solve the need to optimize the existing operational vector index. So, this option is incorrect.

choices.text: Reconfigure the OpenSearch cluster with additional shards, optimized replica counts, and suitable instance types, and tune ANN parameters such as ef_search and ef_construction
choices.explanation: Reconfigure the OpenSearch cluster with additional shards, optimized replica counts, and suitable instance types, and tune ANN parameters such as ef_search and ef_construction Tuning shard counts and replica configurations helps distribute vector search workloads across the cluster, improving concurrency and reducing hot shard issues. Selecting larger or more appropriate instance types increases CPU and memory capacity, which is particularly important for ANN based vector search workloads. Optimizing ANN parameters such as ef_search and ef_construction improves recall and latency trade offs, allowing teams to balance performance and accuracy based on API requirements. This combination directly aligns with the need to optimize performance at scale for high concurrency vector workloads. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/amazon-opensearch-services-vector-database-capabilities-explained/

choices.text: Reduce the number of shards to a single global shard and disable replicas to consolidate compute resources onto fewer nodes
choices.explanation: Using a single shard with no replicas concentrates all search traffic on one node, which severely restricts concurrency and increases latency. This approach also reduces resilience and fails to meet performance needs for high traffic recommendation APIs.

choices.text: Segment the dataset into multiple specialized indices based on product category and apply ANN tuning and shard resizing per index
choices.explanation: Segment the dataset into multiple specialized indices based on product category and apply ANN tuning and shard resizing per index Splitting the dataset into category based indices reduces the size of each index and limits the search scope, which significantly improves query performance. Each index can then be tuned independently for shard count, replicas, and ANN parameters, which gives the team granular control over performance.

choices.text: Replace ANN based search with exact k-NN search on the existing single index and increase cluster storage capacity
choices.explanation: This option is incorrect because replacing ANN with exact k-NN significantly increases computational cost and query latency for an 8-million-vector index under high concurrency. Exact k-NN does not scale efficiently at this dataset size and removes the ability to tune ANN parameters such as ef_search or ef_construction, which are critical for optimizing performance. Simply increasing storage does not address CPU, memory, or shard-level bottlenecks, so this approach fails to resolve the underlying latency issues in the recommendation API.

questions.question: A startup is building a multi tenant software as a service knowledge assistant that uses retrieval augmented generation on Amazon Bedrock to provide customer specific answers. Each customer uploads proprietary documents that the system stores as embeddings inside a vector database for semantic search. The platform must scale to thousands of tenants while ensuring strict data isolation, encryption, secure cross tenant access control, and predictable performance for each customer. What is the most effective way to architect the multi tenant vector storage and isolation model?

choices.text: Export all tenant documents to Amazon S3 and use a centralized Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base to generate embeddings for every tenant in one unified vector store. Then apply metadata based filtering inside the Knowledge Base to ensure that queries return only tenant relevant results
choices.explanation: Placing all tenant data into a single Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base centralizes embeddings and increases the risk of cross tenant exposure through misconfigured metadata filters. It also prevents independent scaling or performance tuning per tenant.

choices.text: Store all tenants' embeddings in a single shared OpenSearch Serverless vector collection and tag each vector with a tenant ID for filtering. Then rely on client side application logic to filter out results that do not match the active tenant
choices.explanation: Storing all embeddings in a single shared vector collection and filtering by tenant ID creates a high risk of accidental data leakage because isolation depends entirely on application logic. This design does not take advantage of native data isolation patterns provided by OpenSearch Serverless and violates multi tenant security best practices.

choices.text: Create dedicated vector collections or namespaces per tenant using Amazon OpenSearch Serverless and enforce tenant isolation with resource policies, encryption by default, and fine grained IAM controls. Then route all customer queries through a multi tenant aware retrieval layer that selects the correct collection based on the authenticated tenant identity
choices.explanation: Create dedicated vector collections or namespaces per tenant using Amazon OpenSearch Serverless and enforce tenant isolation with resource policies, encryption by default, and fine grained IAM controls. Then route all customer queries through a multi tenant aware retrieval layer that selects the correct collection based on the authenticated tenant identity This solution uses Amazon OpenSearch Serverless multi tenant capabilities where each tenant receives its own isolated vector collection, providing strict separation of data and search operations. Resource policies and encryption by default ensure that only the intended tenant can access its collection, while IAM based access control prevents cross tenant leakage. A multi tenant retrieval layer uses authenticated tenant context to route queries to the correct vector collection. This design aligns with the requirements for secure vector storage, scalable semantic search, and predictable performance across thousands of customers. Build a multi-tenant serverless architecture in Amazon OpenSearch Service: via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/patterns/build-a-multi-tenant-serverless-architecture-in-amazon-opensearch-service.html

choices.text: Deploy a separate Aurora PostgreSQL cluster with pgvector for each tenant so embeddings remain fully isolated at the database level. Then integrate each cluster with a shared retrieval service that connects to the correct cluster based on the tenant identifier
choices.explanation: Provisioning a separate Aurora pgvector cluster per tenant guarantees strong isolation but does not scale to thousands of customers and increases operational overhead significantly. This architecture contradicts the goal of centralizing infrastructure while keeping costs and management tasks low.

questions.question: A global ecommerce company is deploying a GenAI powered helpdesk assistant on Amazon Bedrock to handle order, refund, and shipping inquiries across multiple regions. The compliance team requires that the assistant always follow a company approved professional tone, avoid legal advice, and return responses using a strict JSON output schema that downstream systems can reliably process. The engineering team wants a centralized way to define system prompts, follow governance norms, apply behavioral constraints, and enforce consistent output formatting across all microservices that invoke the foundation model. How would you design the model instruction framework and service integration to enforce these requirements across all applications? (Select two)

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to define parameterized system prompts, apply JSON Schema based output templates, and configure approval workflows
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to define parameterized system prompts, apply JSON Schema based output templates, and configure approval workflows Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management provides a centralized platform to define system prompts, parameterized templates, and JSON Schema based output structures that every microservice can consistently apply. This ensures that the helpdesk assistant always uses the same role instructions, tone guidelines, and behavioral constraints, regardless of which backend service initiates the foundation model call. Version control and approval workflows allow prompt authors, reviewers, and administrators to collaboratively manage updates while maintaining strict governance over production prompts.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management to host all prompt templates but allow microservices to automatically promote template versions as part of their CI deployments
choices.explanation: This approach is incorrect because automatic template promotion bypasses required governance and violates approval workflows.

choices.text: Use DynamoDB to store JSON schemas and rely on Lambda functions to inject tone guidelines and filter prohibited content before returning responses. Use Step Functions to orchestrate prompt selection logic for each workflow
choices.explanation: DynamoDB, Lambda, and Step Functions cannot replace the governance and template management features provided by Bedrock Prompt Management. Injecting tone or filtering logic through Lambda introduces inconsistencies and increases operational overhead.

choices.text: Apply Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to enforce tone requirements and block legal advisory content across all model interactions
choices.explanation: Apply Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to enforce tone requirements and block legal advisory content across all model interactions Amazon Bedrock Guardrails complements this by enforcing safety and content policies such as blocking legal advisory content and maintaining the required professional tone. Guardrails apply uniformly across all FM interactions and remove the burden of building custom filtering logic inside each service. Together, these capabilities create a reliable, compliant, and scalable model instruction framework for enterprise wide GenAI workloads. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html

choices.text: Store all prompt templates in S3 and use IAM policies to restrict which microservices can modify them. Use CloudWatch Logs to review prompt usage and ensure that applications follow the JSON response format
choices.explanation: Storing prompts in S3 and controlling access with IAM does not provide centralized versioning, parameterization, or approval workflows. CloudWatch Logs can record usage but cannot enforce tone rules or guarantee JSON formatted outputs across applications.

questions.question: A compliance team needs to process tens of thousands of PDF reports each night and generate concise GenAI summaries for auditors. The reports are uploaded to Amazon S3 throughout the day, and the summaries must be ready by the next morning. The team wants to avoid long synchronous API calls, handle failures for individual documents, and automatically retry only failed items without reprocessing the whole batch. How should the solutions architect orchestrate this large scale, batch GenAI summarization workflow?

choices.text: Create a nightly Step Functions Express workflow that uses a single Lambda task to scan S3 for all new PDFs, call Bedrock in large batches, and write all summaries back to S3. The workflow uses a Retry policy on that Lambda task so any error causes the entire batch job to rerun from the beginning.
choices.explanation: This option uses a Step Functions Express workflow but processes all documents inside a single Lambda task that scans S3 and calls Bedrock in bulk. While Express workflows can handle high throughput, a single task that batches tens of thousands of documents ties the fate of the entire job to one function and one retry policy; any failure forces a rerun of the whole batch instead of retrying only the problematic items. It does not exploit the per item parallelization and fine grained retry capabilities that a Map state in a Standard workflow can provide for large nightly batches.

choices.text: Configure Amazon S3 to send document upload events to SQS and use an EventBridge schedule to start a Step Functions Standard workflow that consumes the queue each night with a Map state over document identifiers. Each Map item invokes Lambda to fetch the PDF from S3, calls Amazon Bedrock for summarization, writes the summary back to S3, and applies per item retry policies so only failed documents run again
choices.explanation: Configure Amazon S3 to send document upload events to SQS and use an EventBridge schedule to start a Step Functions Standard workflow that consumes the queue each night with a Map state over document identifiers. Each Map item invokes Lambda to fetch the PDF from S3, calls Amazon Bedrock for summarization, writes the summary back to S3, and applies per item retry policies so only failed documents run again This solution uses Amazon S3, Amazon SQS, Amazon EventBridge, AWS Step Functions Standard, AWS Lambda, and Amazon Bedrock in a coordinated batch architecture that fits the requirements. S3 event notifications send object created events into SQS, which decouples document ingestion from processing and smooths spiky upload patterns throughout the day. An EventBridge scheduled rule triggers a Step Functions Standard state machine at night, which reads identifiers from SQS or a manifest and uses a Map state to fan out thousands of parallel branches, one per document. Each branch calls a Lambda function that fetches a specific PDF from S3, invokes Bedrock to generate a summary, and stores the result back in S3 for auditors to consume the next morning. Step Functions Map state supports item level retry and error handling, which ensures that only failed documents are retried without reprocessing the successful ones. This pattern provides clear visibility into per document status, integrates well with SQS for decoupling and buffering, and uses Bedrock asynchronously through Lambda while avoiding long running synchronous calls from clients. It also leverages the durability and orchestration guarantees of Step Functions Standard workflows, which are designed for long running, high fan out batch processing with strong observability. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/state-map.html

choices.text: Configure S3 event notifications to trigger a Lambda function for every new PDF and have the function call Bedrock synchronously to generate a summary and store it in S3
choices.explanation: This option triggers a Lambda per document on every S3 upload and calls Bedrock synchronously, which effectively makes each summarization a small, isolated operation without any centralized orchestration for the nightly batch. It can quickly run into concurrency management challenges, offers limited per item observability across tens of thousands of functions, and does not give the team a straightforward way to coordinate retries for only failed documents in the context of a scheduled nightly run.

choices.text: Build an AWS Glue job that runs each night, crawls the S3 bucket for new PDFs, and calls Bedrock from within a single Spark driver script that loops over all documents. The script writes summaries back to S3 and reruns the entire Glue job if any error appears in the nightly logs
choices.explanation: This option centralizes all work inside a single AWS Glue job that loops over every PDF and calls Bedrock from one script, which creates a monolithic nightly batch. If any document triggers an exception and the Glue job fails, the team must rerun the entire job, which reprocesses already completed documents and wastes time and compute. It does not offer fine grained, per document retry semantics or a clean parallel orchestration model for tens of thousands of independent summarization tasks.

questions.question: A rapidly growing social learning platform allows students to collaborate through shared discussion threads, AI assisted study groups, and problem solving sessions. The safety team has reported increasing volumes of harmful prompts, toxic interactions, and attempts to manipulate model behavior through cleverly crafted inputs. Leadership wants an end to end safety pipeline that filters user input before it reaches the model, enforces model level guardrails during generation, and validates responses again before returning them to users. Which options do you recommend? (Select two)

choices.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to pre screen and classify harmful or unsafe user inputs before they reach the model. Apply Amazon Bedrock Guardrails during model invocation to enforce policy based filtering across sensitive content dimensions
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Comprehend to pre screen and classify harmful or unsafe user inputs before they reach the model. Apply Amazon Bedrock Guardrails during model invocation to enforce policy based filtering across sensitive content dimensions Amazon Comprehend provides accurate classification of harmful, toxic, or policy violating text, which allows the system to block unsafe inputs before they are ever processed by the model. This pre processing step significantly reduces the risk of harmful prompts being executed, since it intercepts and filters problems at the earliest stage. Applying Amazon Bedrock Guardrails during model invocation creates a second safety layer that monitors and filters content inside the model pipeline. This combination ensures that even if a harmful input bypasses pre screening, the guardrails still control what the model is allowed to generate, delivering strong in model protection. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to retrieve safe reference material and instruct the model to generate responses based on trusted content, then use API Gateway to enforce simple schema validation. Leverage prompts and retrieval guidance to lower the risk of harmful interactions
choices.explanation: Grounding through Knowledge Bases helps with factual consistency but does not provide strong safeguards for detecting toxic or harmful interactions. Schema validation in API Gateway is limited to structure and cannot enforce content safety or block inappropriate model outputs.

choices.text: Use a Lambda function to perform validation on model outputs, checking for safety compliance and filtering out any unsafe content. Configure API Gateway to enforce final response filtering so that only validated responses are returned to end users
choices.explanation: Use a Lambda function to perform validation on model outputs, checking for safety compliance and filtering out any unsafe content. Configure API Gateway to enforce final response filtering so that only validated responses are returned to end users A Lambda based post processing validator introduces an additional checkpoint that examines the generated response before it is delivered. This step ensures that all outputs conform to safety policies and allows developers to add custom logic for domain specific safety rules. Finally, API Gateway provides a hardened enforcement layer by performing final response validation and blocking content that fails safety checks. This ensures that only fully validated, policy compliant responses reach end users, completing a multi layer defense in depth safety architecture.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to filter inputs and outputs, use CloudWatch alarms to detect potentially harmful interactions. Add a Lambda function that logs unsafe events for later analysis
choices.explanation: Relying entirely on Bedrock Guardrails with CloudWatch alarms creates a reactive system that detects issues only after unsafe content has already reached users. Logging and alerting help with monitoring but do not provide layered protection across pre processing and post processing stages.

choices.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to classify harmful content and send only clean inputs to the model, then invoke the model without guardrails and use S3 based logging to monitor output patterns. Add a Step Functions workflow to periodically review logs for safety violations
choices.explanation: Using Comprehend without model guardrails creates significant exposure because the model may still generate unsafe content even with clean inputs. Reviewing logs periodically does not prevent real time harmful interactions in an active social learning environment.

questions.question: A media intelligence company processes millions of global news articles each day and provides real-time insights to analysts, advertisers, and policy research teams. To support fast and accurate information retrieval, the platform needs a vector search solution that can segment content by topic, combine semantic relevance with keyword precision, and accelerate lookup using approximate nearest neighbor search. The engineering team must also tune index mappings, shard counts, and replica strategies to handle query spikes during major events. Which solution should the team choose to meet these requirements?

choices.text: Store all documents in a Bedrock Knowledge Base and rely on metadata tagging to differentiate topics and improve retrieval quality. Use the built-in retrieval flow to handle semantic relevance and indexing requirements
choices.explanation: Bedrock Knowledge Bases offers managed vector storage and chunking but does not provide the degree of control needed for index mappings, shard configurations, or performance tuning. It also does not support hybrid semantic plus keyword search workflows required for news analysis at this scale.

choices.text: Use Amazon Athena federated queries to search enriched metadata and perform semantic lookups through an external embedding API
choices.explanation: You can run Athena federated queries to filter articles based on metadata fields and forward the results to an external service for semantic similarity scoring. However, this design does not provide vector search, ANN indexing, hybrid retrieval, or shard level performance tuning, which are all core requirements in the scenario. Athena can filter metadata but cannot replace OpenSearch based semantic search workflows or provide sub-second vector lookup performance at scale.

choices.text: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service and store all documents in a single global index with default mappings and no topic-specific partitioning. Create one large index for all news articles and rely on OpenSearch defaults for mapping, shard configurations, and keyword fields. Use exact k-NN search across the entire dataset for semantic retrieval
choices.explanation: Storing millions of documents in one global index without custom mappings, topic segmentation, or ANN optimization will cause latency issues during peak traffic. This configuration ignores the need for hybrid retrieval and advanced performance tuning features.

choices.text: Configure Amazon OpenSearch Service with the Neural Search plugin. Deploy the Neural Search plugin to enable hybrid semantic and keyword retrieval across topic-specific indices. Configure ANN-based acceleration, shard tuning, and optimized mappings to reduce latency under concurrent load
choices.explanation: Configure Amazon OpenSearch Service with the Neural Search plugin. Deploy the Neural Search plugin to enable hybrid semantic and keyword retrieval across topic-specific indices. Configure ANN-based acceleration, shard tuning, and optimized mappings to reduce latency under concurrent load The Neural Search plugin allows OpenSearch to perform hybrid semantic and keyword retrieval using ANN acceleration, which significantly improves performance for large scale workloads like news analytics. It also provides advanced tuning options such as custom index mappings, shard allocation strategies, and topic-based segmentation for improved query locality and latency. This configuration meets all requirements because it supports vector search, topic-based partitioning, hybrid retrieval, and optimized ANN search while giving architects full control over performance tuning. It ensures the system scales efficiently as the number of news articles grows. via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/big-data/power-neural-search-with-ai-ml-connectors-in-amazon-opensearch-service/

questions.question: A legal tech platform wants to measure accuracy, robustness and reasoning quality across multiple model candidates. The team must approve new foundation models into production only after they pass a consistent and repeatable evaluation cycle. They need an automated mechanism that applies controlled metrics, compares multiple candidates side by side, and produces quantifiable scores for decision making. Which evaluation approach is most suitable for these requirements?

choices.text: Use an internal human review process where domain experts score outputs from each model candidate. Store reviewer feedback in a structured format and compute aggregate ratings before approving models for production
choices.explanation: Human review introduces subjectivity and inconsistency because reviewers may evaluate similar outputs differently over time. While expert judgment is valuable, it does not provide the repeatability and automated scoring needed for a systematic pre production evaluation pipeline. Human evaluation also does not scale efficiently when the team must assess multiple models or large numbers of prompts.

choices.text: Use CloudWatch Logs and embed evaluation metadata inside inference logs so that engineers can manually compare hallucination incidents and summarization differences across models. Generate nightly reports from the aggregated logs to decide which model to promote
choices.explanation: CloudWatch Logs can store inference related data but they are not designed to compute accuracy, robustness and reasoning quality. Any evaluation logic would have to be implemented manually and would lack the consistency and standardization required for model to model comparison. This approach increases operational burden and reduces reliability in evaluation outcomes.

choices.text: Use a Lambda powered A/B testing framework that routes traffic between multiple model candidates and collects user feedback at runtime. Use this feedback to compute the metrics and choose the highest rated model for production approval
choices.explanation: A Lambda based A/B testing framework relies on live user traffic to evaluate model quality, which does not match the requirement for pre production evaluation. This approach exposes users to unapproved models and does not guarantee consistent scoring across multiple candidates.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation to automatically score multiple model candidates against a curated evaluation dataset
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation to automatically score multiple model candidates against a curated evaluation dataset Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation provides a managed, automatic, and repeatable evaluation framework that compares multiple foundation model candidates using consistent datasets and scoring methodologies. It allows the legal tech team to measure accuracy, robustness and reasoning quality through structured evaluation tasks. The service supports custom evaluation datasets, enabling the team to incorporate legal domain specific prompts and reference answers for precise scoring. Because the evaluation runs in a controlled and repeatable environment, the platform gains clear and measurable insights into model performance differences. Bedrock Model Evaluation also produces quantitative scores and ranking information, making it easier to determine whether a model meets production thresholds for legal accuracy and reliability. This is essential for high stakes environments where hallucinations or factual inconsistencies can lead to compliance issues. The automated nature of the service ensures that every model candidate goes through the same rigorous evaluation pipeline before deployment. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/evaluation.html

questions.question: A financial services company is building a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) system on Amazon Bedrock. New policy documents arrive in a central S3 bucket multiple times per day. Each document must pass through a pipeline that performs text extraction, PII redaction, chunking, embedding generation, and index updates in a vector database. The development team wants a repeatable, event driven orchestration pattern that separates ingestion from query time and can be versioned as the pipeline evolves. Which orchestration strategy would you use to implement and manage this GenAI ingestion workflow?

choices.text: Configures S3 event notifications to trigger a Step Functions Standard workflow that orchestrates Lambda tasks for text extraction, PII redaction, chunking, Bedrock embedding generation, and index updates in OpenSearch Serverless. Manage the Step Functions definition using AWS CloudFormation, which supports versioning and allows controlled rollout of new pipeline designs
choices.explanation: Configures S3 event notifications to trigger a Step Functions Standard workflow that orchestrates Lambda tasks for text extraction, PII redaction, chunking, Bedrock embedding generation, and index updates in OpenSearch Serverless. Manage the Step Functions definition using AWS CloudFormation, which supports versioning and allows controlled rollout of new pipeline designs This solution uses Amazon S3 event notifications to trigger an AWS Step Functions Standard state machine that orchestrates a sequence of Lambda tasks for extraction, PII redaction, chunking, embedding calls to Amazon Bedrock, and index updates in a vector store such as Amazon OpenSearch Serverless. S3 events provide the event driven entry point whenever new policy documents land in the bucket, while Step Functions offers a visual and versionable workflow definition that clearly models each stage of the ingestion pipeline. Standard workflows are appropriate for longer running and durable jobs, and they integrate directly with Bedrock through optimized service integrations so you can invoke embedding models from inside the state machine. By defining each transformation step as its own Lambda task and connecting them in a state machine, the team can apply per step retries, error handling, and branching logic as the pipeline evolves. The team can manage the Step Functions definition with infrastructure as code such as AWS CloudFormation, which supports versioning and controlled rollout of new pipeline designs while keeping query time retrieval logic separate from ingestion. This pattern aligns with AWS guidance for orchestrating multi step generative AI and RAG workflows using Step Functions and Bedrock, while OpenSearch Serverless handles vector search over the embedded chunks.

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Agent to handle both ingestion and query flows in the same agent, allowing the agent to read from S3, generate embeddings, and update the vector database whenever users submit queries. Maintain the entire pipeline logic in the agent configuration
choices.explanation: This option uses Amazon Bedrock Agents as the only orchestration mechanism and mixes ingestion concerns with query time interactions inside a single conversational agent. Bedrock Agents are designed primarily for interactive, multi turn experiences and tool calling at query time, not for long running offline ingestion pipelines that process every document as it lands in S3. Keeping ingestion logic inside the agent configuration also makes it harder to treat the ingestion flow as a separately versioned, event driven pipeline that operates independently from user queries.

choices.text: Build a single nightly AWS Glue job that scans the S3 bucket for new policy documents, performs text extraction and PII redaction inside a Spark script, calls Bedrock embeddings for each chunk, and then writes vectors directly into the index. Rerun the same Glue job to fix issues in the pipeline
choices.explanation: This option centralizes the entire ingestion process inside a single nightly AWS Glue job that loops over all new documents, performs transformations, and calls Bedrock embeddings from within a Spark script. Although Glue can handle ETL style processing on data in S3, it does not naturally provide the fine grained, event driven orchestration per document that S3 triggered Step Functions workflows offer, and it ties versioning of the pipeline to a monolithic job script. The approach focuses on a batch style scan rather than a repeatable event driven pattern where each object arrival can trigger a consistent, versioned ingestion workflow that keeps query time RAG logic decoupled.

choices.text: Set up an Amazon Kendra index to directly crawl the S3 bucket on a frequent schedule and leverage the built-in extraction and indexing features. Update relevance tuning and capacity settings on the index when new document types or policies appear
choices.explanation: This option uses Amazon Kendra to crawl the S3 bucket on a schedule and rely on its built in extraction and indexing, which can work well for many enterprise search scenarios. However, it does not fully express a custom, versioned pipeline that performs explicit PII redaction, controlled chunking strategies, and Bedrock embedding generation into an external vector database as described in the scenario. It also couples ingestion more tightly to the Kendra index configuration instead of modeling the ingestion workflow itself as a separate, event driven orchestrated pipeline.

questions.question: A government agency needs a Policy Drafting Assistant that can synthesize complex legal materials such as statutes, regulations, and internal policy memos into grounded draft proposals. The system must run entirely inside AWS GovCloud to meet strict data sovereignty obligations, and it must support asynchronous or batch style workloads so analysts can request long form policy drafts without waiting for interactive responses. The agency also requires full traceability and grounded reasoning so that reviewers can verify the source of each generated insight. Additionally, the platform must minimize operational overhead and remain cost efficient because budgets for infrastructure operations are limited. Which combination of services and architectural choices should the agency use to meet these requirements? (Select two)

choices.text: Use AWS Step Functions to orchestrate a multi step workflow that performs manual retrieval from S3, constructs prompts inside Lambda functions, and then calls the Bedrock InvokeModel API. Split long running workloads into multiple Step Functions states to generate drafts in segments
choices.explanation: Step Functions can orchestrate multiple steps but they are not optimized for grounding or long running text generation. Draft generation workflows that span thousands of tokens are not naturally suited to Step Functions state transitions because state boundaries are not designed for large context model invocations. This approach forces prompt assembly and retrieval logic into Lambda functions, creating duplicated logic and increasing operational complexity.

choices.text: Use Titan Text models available in AWS GovCloud together with Amazon Bedrock asynchronous model invocation to run long form batch generation jobs
choices.explanation: Use Titan Text models available in AWS GovCloud together with Amazon Bedrock asynchronous model invocation to run long form batch generation jobs

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to ground outputs in statutes and internal memoranda, ensuring traceability and contextual relevance
choices.explanation: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to ground outputs in statutes and internal memoranda, ensuring traceability and contextual relevance This combination of options meets the requirements by using Titan Text models that are supported in AWS GovCloud. These models can run entirely within GovCloud boundaries, satisfying data sovereignty rules. Amazon Bedrock asynchronous invocation is suited for long form policy drafting because it handles extended generation times without tying up interactive execution paths. Knowledge Bases in GovCloud allow the agency to ingest statutes, regulations, and internal memos so that the model can ground its outputs in authoritative sources. This grounding improves reliability and supports the requirement for traceable reasoning where reviewers can verify citations. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html

choices.text: Host a large fine tuned LLM on Amazon SageMaker GPU instances in AWS GovCloud and process batch jobs using SageMaker Processing. Use custom logic inside the inference container to perform grounding by searching static documents stored in S3 and embedding them into prompts for draft generation
choices.explanation: While SageMaker is available in GovCloud, fine tuning and hosting a large LLM introduces significant operational responsibilities. The agency would need to manage GPU infrastructure, autoscaling, inference container logic, and retrieval code, which increases cost and contradicts the requirement for low operational complexity. Grounding through manual search and prompt assembly is error prone and does not offer the managed retrieval capabilities available through Bedrock Knowledge Bases.

choices.text: Deploy an ECS cluster running custom containers that include open source LLMs and mount encrypted EFS volumes containing legal materials. Use an internal API Gateway endpoint to submit batch requests and stream outputs back to analysts through a custom response handler
choices.explanation: Running open source LLMs in ECS containers requires the agency to maintain container images, cluster configurations, scaling policies, and integration logic for storage and inference. This adds a heavy operational burden that conflicts with the stated requirement to minimize infrastructure management. It also lacks the fully managed grounding and orchestration capabilities that Bedrock Knowledge Bases and Bedrock asynchronous invocation provide.

questions.question: An internal policy chatbot ingests long HR documents and meeting transcripts, but users report that answers often ignore the most recent amendments that appear near the end of the source files. The ingestion pipeline currently uses a fixed-size, naive chunking strategy across all document types. As a GenAI engineer, how should you redesign the document chunking and context construction approach so that relevant content is preserved while staying within model limits?

choices.text: Implement Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with hierarchical and overlap aware chunking, and configure metadata tagging so that recent amendments receive higher retrieval scores. Use context window diagnostics and Bedrock RAG performance metrics to verify that end sections are preserved during context assembly
choices.explanation: Implement Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with hierarchical and overlap aware chunking, and configure metadata tagging so that recent amendments receive higher retrieval scores. Use context window diagnostics and Bedrock RAG performance metrics to verify that end sections are preserved during context assembly Using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with hierarchical and overlap aware chunking ensures that segment boundaries follow the natural document structure, which improves the preservation of critical amendments at the end of long files. Metadata driven ranking allows the system to bias retrieval toward the most recent changes without depending on fixed chunk sizes. Context window diagnostics and retrieval evaluation tools in Bedrock help confirm that the generated context includes both earlier and later sections of the document in a balanced way. This approach eliminates the loss of tail end amendments and maintains model token constraints. How content chunking works for knowledge bases: via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/kb-chunking.html

choices.text: Use Amazon Textract to convert the documents, then rely on default Bedrock model streaming to automatically adjust context inclusion for longer documents. Add more few shot examples in the system prompt to guide the model to consider end sections in its responses
choices.explanation: Streaming output from Bedrock models does not influence how context is selected or assembled, and it cannot compensate for missing content caused by naive chunking. Converting documents with Textract and adding more few shot examples in the system prompt will not fix retrieval gaps introduced earlier in the ingestion pipeline.

choices.text: Apply AWS Glue jobs to reformat documents into larger uniform chunks and use Amazon Kendra without metadata ranking to retrieve them as complete blocks. Increase the model max tokens setting so that more content can fit into the prompt without adjusting the ingestion strategy
choices.explanation: Reformatting documents into larger uniform chunks increases the likelihood of dropping important segments because uniform blocks rarely match meaningful boundaries in complex documents. Increasing model token limits does not address retrieval quality issues and cannot ensure that the most relevant amendments are selected.

choices.text: Adopt Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with simple flat chunking and configure a retrieval filter based only on document timestamps. Rely on a larger top K value in the retriever to compensate for missed amendments during context construction
choices.explanation: A flat chunking strategy and reliance on top K retrieval cannot guarantee that recent amendments are returned, because retrieval filters based only on timestamps do not address structural segmentation issues. Without hierarchical chunking or overlap adjustments, important tail sections can still be lost during preprocessing.

questions.question: A global legal consultancy is developing an AI driven clause drafting system that must route every generated clause through mandatory human review. The firm wants attorneys to evaluate each draft, provide structured feedback, and either approve or request revisions. The firm also wants to capture feedback metadata so it can analyze common revision categories, reviewer patterns, and long term trends that influence model improvement. To achieve this, the system must coordinate automated drafting steps, route human review tasks, trigger downstream processing, and store structured review data in a repository suitable for long term analysis. What do you suggest?

choices.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Agents to manage the drafting and review process and configure Amazon Augmented AI (A2I) only to notify attorneys by email when a clause is ready for review. Store each reviewer comment as a separate S3 object for later analysis
choices.explanation: Using Bedrock Agents to manage the entire review flow bypasses structured human review capabilities provided by A2I. Storing reviewer feedback in S3 creates unstructured data that does not support attribute level indexing or efficient trend analysis, which the firm requires for long term insights.
