questions.question: A large healthcare provider is introducing an AI system that helps generate clinical summaries by reviewing doctor notes, patient history, and recent interactions. These summaries must be produced reliably because they support real clinical decision making, but the compliance and operations teams are concerned about potential risks. They worry that the AI could enter repetitive reasoning cycles, continue processing far longer than intended, or fail unpredictably if supporting systems become unavailable during execution. They want a tightly controlled workflow that can automatically stop when necessary and prevent cascading failures during periods of instability. What do you recommend?

choices.text: Design a safeguarded workflow using AWS Step Functions with state timeouts and Lambda functions that include retry logic, and configure CloudWatch alarms to send notifications when error rates rise. Allow the workflow to continue running while alarms are active and rely on SNS notifications instead of halting execution
choices.explanation: This option does not implement a circuit breaker that actually stops execution when downstream systems fail. Allowing the workflow to continue running during high error periods exposes the system to cascading failures and does not meet the requirement for automatic workflow termination.

choices.text: Build a workflow using Amazon Bedrock Agents and configure long agent session windows to accommodate extended reasoning cycles. Rely on agent driven retry mechanisms to handle unavailable downstream systems without implementing a centralized circuit breaker or stopping conditions
choices.explanation: Extending agent sessions and relying on agent level retries does not address risks related to infinite loops, runaway execution, or cascading failures. Without workflow level stopping conditions, circuit breakers, and timeouts, failures in downstream systems can cause unpredictable behavior.

choices.text: Use Step Functions with a single global timeout for the entire workflow and rely on CloudWatch metrics for monitoring. Allow downstream API calls to continue retrying within the state machine without any stopping conditions
choices.explanation: A single global timeout does not prevent iterative retry loops or protect the workflow from repeated downstream failures. Without explicit stopping conditions and automated circuit breaker enforcement, the system cannot safely halt execution during an outage.

choices.text: Design a safeguarded workflow using AWS Step Functions with explicit stopping conditions and state timeouts, apply strict Lambda function timeouts, enforce least privilege IAM policies, and configure a circuit breaker pattern using CloudWatch alarms that halts the workflow when error thresholds are exceeded. Implement logic that stops execution immediately if downstream systems fail or responses become unreliable
choices.explanation: Design a safeguarded workflow using AWS Step Functions with explicit stopping conditions and state timeouts, apply strict Lambda function timeouts, enforce least privilege IAM policies, and configure a circuit breaker pattern using CloudWatch alarms that halts the workflow when error thresholds are exceeded. Implement logic that stops execution immediately if downstream systems fail or responses become unreliable This solution uses a comprehensive safeguard strategy that protects the workflow at every layer. Step Functions stopping conditions and state timeouts ensure that the AI never enters uncontrolled loops or runs longer than allowed. Lambda timeouts prevent individual tasks from consuming excessive compute or blocking workflow progress. Least privilege IAM policies restrict what the AI can access, limiting compliance risk and reducing the chance of unintended actions. CloudWatch alarms combined with circuit breaker logic provide a final layer of defense by stopping the workflow entirely when error rates spike or downstream systems become unstable. This multi layer safety model produces predictable behavior, protects against cascading failures, and aligns with healthcare compliance requirements. Circuit breaker pattern implementation using AWS services: via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/cloud-design-patterns/circuit-breaker.html

questions.question: A content moderation team performs nightly processing of millions of user posts using a large language model to detect policy violations. The workload is heavy but not user facing, and completing it within several hours is acceptable. Their current real time endpoint scales sharply during the batch window, causing high costs and occasional throttling. The team wants a design that can absorb very high volumes of requests, smooth out load, and better align infrastructure usage with the relaxed latency requirements of this batch job. How should you redesign the deployment to handle this large batch workload more efficiently?

choices.text: Replace the current endpoint with Amazon Bedrock provisioned throughput so that the model has dedicated capacity and can process all posts in real time during the batch window. Increase provisioned units during peak times and rely on CloudWatch alarms to notify the team if throttling occurs
choices.explanation: Provisioned throughput on a fully managed model API is optimized for predictable, steady traffic and low latency requirements, not for very large batch jobs that can take hours to complete. Scaling provisioned capacity up for a short nightly window is likely to be expensive, and treating a flexible batch workload as if it were an interactive real time use case does not take advantage of the relaxed latency constraints available here.

choices.text: Keep the existing SageMaker AI real time endpoint and front it with an Amazon SQS queue that a fleet of Lambda functions drains by sending synchronous requests to the endpoint. Rely on Auto Scaling to expand the endpoint instance count rapidly during the nightly batch window to avoid throttling
choices.explanation: Putting an SQS queue in front of a synchronous real time endpoint does not change the fundamental cost and scaling profile of the endpoint, which still needs to handle large, concurrent request bursts during the batch window. Lambda workers that drain the queue will generate concentrated traffic, causing the endpoint to scale to many instances and potentially hit throttling limits, which recreates the original problem rather than solving it.

choices.text: Configure a SageMaker AI asynchronous inference endpoint that reads input payloads from Amazon S3 and processes moderation requests in the background, returning results to an S3 output location. Use a queue or scheduled process to upload nightly batches so that the endpoint scales gradually and optimizes instance utilization for long running jobs
choices.explanation: Configure a SageMaker AI asynchronous inference endpoint that reads input payloads from Amazon S3 and processes moderation requests in the background, returning results to an S3 output location. Use a queue or scheduled process to upload nightly batches so that the endpoint scales gradually and optimizes instance utilization for long running jobs Asynchronous inference in SageMaker AI is designed for large batch or non interactive workloads where individual requests do not need an immediate response. By writing inputs to Amazon S3 and allowing the asynchronous endpoint to pull and process them in the background, the system can smooth out load, reduce the need for high instance counts, and avoid per request throttling spikes that occur with synchronous endpoints. Using a queue or scheduled uploader to send nightly batches into S3 lets the team tune concurrency and scaling behavior to match the available compute capacity and cost targets. Output written back to S3 can be consumed later by downstream pipelines or analytics jobs, making this pattern ideal for heavy, time flexible moderation workloads that only need to complete within a broad processing window. via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/async-inference.html

choices.text: Increase the instance size and maximum instance count for the current SageMaker AI real time endpoint and configure aggressive scale out policies during the nightly processing window. Use reserved instances or savings plans to try to mitigate the higher cost of running large instances for extended periods
choices.explanation: Simply increasing instance size and maximum instance count on a synchronous real time endpoint forces the system to provision more expensive capacity during the batch window, which raises cost without addressing the mismatch between interactive infrastructure and batch processing. Aggressive scale out policies can reduce throttling but will amplify surprise bills when millions of posts trigger rapid horizontal scaling on large instances.
